EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued melting of the remaining snowpack prolonged flooding concerns through the Milk River and Poplar River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuing the flooding from the end of March, recent melting of the larger snowpack allowed the Tampico river gauge reading (TMPM8) along the Milk River to remain above the flood stage of 23.0 into the first week of April. The river dropped back down below flood stage at 5:52 AM MST on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After recent rainfall, clearing skies and calm winds allowed dense fog to form over Phillips County during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system was accompanied by unusually-high amounts of moisture, periods of steady of heavy rain, and some thunderstorms over north-central Montana during May 25th through the early morning of the 28th. In addition, relatively-high snow levels allowed rain to fall on lingering snowpack along the Rocky Mountain Front, where easterly upslope flow enhanced rainfall totals. Portions of the Rocky Mountain Front received as much as around 3 to 6 inches of total rainfall. Runoff from snowmelt and significant rainfall led to flooding along some rivers and overland flooding. The worst flooding was focused in the Augusta area, along the Sun River, and along Badger Creek in the Browning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fergus County Sheriff reported flooding near Utica. Exact duration of flooding unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle shortwave trough drifted southeastward over north-central and southwest Montana during the afternoon and early evening of May 30th. Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed via daytime heating of higher terrain amidst weak to moderate instability and weak vertical wind shear. One thunderstorm managed to produce damaging wind gusts near Helena.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a shed was damaged and displaced 10-yards by outflow. Photos were provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface pressure gradient between high pressure in southern Idaho and a low pressure system and associated cold front moving southeast in Alberta/Saskatchewan helped strengthen winds for much of the CWA.  Strong winds aloft and static stability at the mountaintop level also helped increase winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. The strongest winds occurred ahead of the aforementioned cold front and continued following the frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT station Monarch Canyon reported a wind gust of 61 mph.

Time-height profiles show 60 knot winds just off the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather vigorous shortwave and attendant arctic cold front traversed the area bringing periods of moderate snow and blowing snow.  The anticipation of blocked upslope flow and other dynamics lead to the issuance of a winter storm warning for Cascade, Gallatin, Judith Basin, and Meagher Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US highway 191 was reportedly closed from Harlo to Eddies Corner due to snow, blowing snow and near zero visibility.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulation was estimated at 10 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skywest Flight 4292 from Salt Lake City to Great Falls was diverted to Bozeman due to snowy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak intrusion of modified arctic air brought heavy snow and gusty northeast winds along the US-2 corridor including Marias Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ten hour period of heavy snow brought 12 inches of snow to Marias Pass on US-2. The Montana Department of Transportation hoisted severe driving conditions between 630 and 9 pm in the evening due to zero visibility from blowing and falling snow. Northeast winds gusting between 20 and 30 mph created blowing and drifting snow during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing road and air temperatures combined with a warm mid-level layer of air caused freezing rain to occur as a Pacific upper level wave moved across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area that saw the most ice accretion was along Interstate 90 east of Lookout Pass towards St. Regis where the Montana Department of Transportation listed severe driving conditions and a local weather station measured 0.15 inches of melted precipitation. Impacts include a jack-knifed tractor trailer west of Saint Regis and the St. Regis schools closed because of the icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific warm front moved over a colder arctic air mass over northwest Montana to bring snow and slick driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow created tough driving conditions in the Flathead Valley which contributed to dozens of accidents. Winds were gusting over 20 mph towards Columbia Falls which created blowing snow and reductions to visibility. Snow amounts ranged from around an inch in Columbia Falls and Flathead Lake to 1.5 inches near Kalispell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT Browning station reported a wind gust of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a large Pacific disturbance increased winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and the adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT Two Medicine Bridge station reported a wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a large Pacific disturbance increased winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and the adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sourdough Ridge station reported a wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A piece of energy splitting from an upper level trough approaching the west coast allowed a surface front to oscillate across the region leading to periods of snow. The snow was heaviest in Gallatin County and then along the plains adjacent to the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total from January 20th through 21st was 7 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT reported a road closure on MT highway 44 MP 0 to 13. This report was relayed through twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface pressure gradient between high pressure in southern Idaho and a low pressure system and associated cold front moving southeast in Alberta/Saskatchewan helped strengthen winds for much of the CWA.  Strong winds aloft and static stability at the mountaintop level also helped increase winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. The strongest winds occurred ahead of the aforementioned cold front and continued following the frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station 3 WNW Ranesford reported a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong jet stream combined with tight surface pressure gradients resulted in high winds across the western half of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts 58 to 64 mph were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post frontal north to northwesterly surface winds were funneled through the Judith Gap resulting in near zero visibilities due to falling/blowing snow.  This set up prompted the issuance of a blizzard warning for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fergus County Sheriff's Office reported the closure of US-191 from Eddies Corner to Harlowton due to zero visibility in blowing and drifting snow. Several vehicles slid off the roadway. This message was relayed via Facebook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters in and near the town of Zortman estimated 6 inches of new snowfall with some drifts in more exposed areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation closed I-94 from Glendive through eastern Dawson County due to severe winter driving conditions.  Snow fall reports of 4 to 5 inches were common across western Dawson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a prolonged cold spell continued, a renewed ice jam and associated flooding affected the Ennis area. In addition, a separate ice jam and related flooding developed along the Gallatin River, near Logan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallatin County Sheriff's Office reported ice jam-related flooding on the Gallatin River, near Logan. Rising floodwaters led to evacuation of at least one residence. Exact duration of flooding unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between late evening of February 8th and late morning of the 9th, an Arctic cold front moved from near the I-90 corridor in western and southwest Montana to a position extending southeastward from northwestern Idaho to east-central Idaho. Behind this front, a strong Arctic high pressure ridge settled southward across north-central and southwest Montana. This ridge was accompanied by a tight surface pressure gradient, especially over southwest Montana and along the Rocky Mountain Front. The tight pressure gradient resulted in breezy surface winds. In turn, the combination of these winds and frigid temperatures yielded warning-criteria wind chills for parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boulder Hill MT DOT station recorded wind chill of -41F with sustained wind of 16 mph. Location: 2-miles SE of Wickes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air, a recent fresh snow cover, and clearing skies all contributed to the coldest temperatures of the season, and the coldest wind chill values for portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures of -15 to -31 and wind speeds of 9 to 18 mph, wind chill values near and colder than -40 were common. The coldest wind chill value of -53 was recorded at 4:15 AM at the Malta airport ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air, a recent fresh snow cover, and clearing skies all contributed to the coldest temperatures of the season, and the coldest wind chill values for portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Combining observations from both Frazer and Glasgow, temperatures in the -20s and wind speeds from 9 to 17 mph combined to create wind chill values in the -40s and -50s in central Valley County.  The coldest wind chill value of -55 was recorded at 12:53 AM at the Glasgow airport ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air, a recent fresh snow cover, and clearing skies all contributed to the coldest temperatures of the season, and the coldest wind chill values for portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures of -20 and below and wind speeds of 9 to 14 mph, wind chill values in the -40s were common across Daniels County.  The coldest wind chill value of -47 was recorded at the Navajo MT-5 DOT site at 7:16 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air, a recent fresh snow cover, and clearing skies all contributed to the coldest temperatures of the season, and the coldest wind chill values for portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -10s and -20s, and wind speeds of 9 to 18 mph, wind chill values in the -40s were common across Sheridan County.  The coldest wind chill value of -48 was recorded at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site at 6:13 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold arctic air, a recent fresh snow cover, and clearing skies all contributed to the coldest temperatures of the season, and the coldest wind chill values for portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Combining multiple observations from across western Roosevelt County, temperatures in the -20s and -30s and wind speeds of 9 to 16 mph, resulted in wind chill values from the -40s to the -60s.  The coldest wind chill value of -66 was recorded at the Poplar RAWS site at 8:18 AM on the 8th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow caused hazardous driving conditions along portions of I-90 and Montana 200 and 83. 17 inches of snow was reported in Potomac, 11 inches in Clinton, and 10 inches in Seeley Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -10s and -20s and wind speeds of 9 to 17 mph, winds chills in the -40s were common across Dawson County.  The coldest wind chill reading of -47 was recorded at 2:21 AM on the 7th at the Lindsay Divide MT-200 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Amid a dominant and persistent arctic air mass and large low pressure trough, gusty east winds and cold night-time temperatures between storms resulted in wind chill values worse than 40 below zero for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -20s and -30s, and wind speeds of 9 to 18 mph, wind chill readings in the -40s and -50s were common across most of Western Roosevelt County from the night of the 14th through the morning of the 15th.  The coldest wind chill reading was -54 degrees at the Poplar RAWS site and was recorded at 5:18 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures hovered in the -10s and as low as -20, and wind speeds persisted from 12 to 19 mph, wind chill values in the -40s were measured at the Sioux Pass MT-16 DOT site during the early morning hours on the 15th.  The coldest wind chill reading of -44 degrees was recorded as 7:03 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-passing trough of low pressure combined with an unstable atmosphere, to form a few strong elevated thunderstorms - one of which became severe with downburst winds in Sheridan County in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported wind gusts up to 65 mph. Time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a 70 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Even though winds were calm, the temperatures alone were near or colder than -40 for most of the night of the 7th and most of the morning of the 8th.  The coldest temperature reading of -44 was recorded at 3:59 AM on the 8th at the Cow Creek MT-13 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued snowmelt, mostly clear skies, and light winds all contributed to the development of dense fog, mainly near and along lower elevation areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced the visibility intermittently to one quarter of a mile or less for one to three hours in the morning on the 22nd from the Wolf Point airport ASOS site to the Poplar airport mesonet site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced the visibility to one quarter of a mile or less for three to four hours in the morning on the 22nd at the Glasgow airport ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent precipitation, cool temperatures, light winds, and partly clear skies all combined to allow some dense fog to form for a few northeast Montana locations near the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced the visibility to less than one quarter of a mile for many hours from the night on the 25th through the morning on the 26th.  Reported by the public through social media. Times estimated by satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent precipitation, cool temperatures, light winds, and partly clear skies all combined to allow some dense fog to form for a few northeast Montana locations near the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog reduced the visibility to one quarter of a mile for two hours in the morning on the 26th at the Scobey airport mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The recent spring season thaw allowed ice along the Missouri River to break up. An ice jam which formed between Wolf Point and Poplar briefly rose the river level above flood stage near Wolf Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of March 29th, at the Wolf Point river forecast point (WPTM8) along the Missouri River, an ice jam caused the river level to briefly rise above the flood stage of 23.0 feet at 4:12 PM MST. The river crested at 23.71 feet at 4:44 PM MST on the 29th. As the ice jam cleared, the river temporarily fell back down below flood stage for just a few minutes before rising above flood stage again. Finally, the river fell below flood stage to end this event at 7:20 PM MST on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the height of the winter storm, wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph with visibilities near zero occurred due to snow and blowing snow.  According to the Montana Department of Transportation travel was severely impacted especially on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture interacted with an intense arctic cold front producing widespread heavy snowfall across areas of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 25th, wind gusts over 40 mph and visibility reduced to less than a quarter of a mile in downtown and east Missoula were observed. Significant travel impacts were reported in the city of Missoula, and Missoula County asked citizens to only travel if absolutely necessary. Flights out of Missoula were also canceled or delayed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture interacted with an intense arctic cold front producing widespread heavy snowfall across areas of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of intense snowfall during the evening hours of the 25th led to significant accumulations and travel impacts. Chains became required over Evaro Hill on US 95 where  24 inches of snow fell. Heavy snow and blowing snow was observed from Clinton to Ninemile, causing impacts to travel on I-90. 11 inches of snow was observed in Alberton and 8 inches was reported in Huson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent cold arctic air mass combined with westerly winds to produce wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower to some northeast Montana locations near the international border with Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures from -17 to -30 and winds speeds of 9 to 16 mph, wind chill values mainly in the -40s persisted for 2 to 4 hours during the early morning on the 2nd through portions of central Valley County. The coldest wind chill reading of -50 was recorded at 5:38 AM at the Saco US-2 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent cold arctic air mass combined with westerly winds to produce wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower to some northeast Montana locations near the international border with Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -20s and winds speeds of 12 to 18 mph, wind chill values of -48 to -50 occurred for about 1 hour during the early morning of the 2nd at the Bluff Creek RAWS site. The coldest wind chill reading of -50 was recorded at 5:44 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Added surface moisture, in part from recent snow melt, combined with clear skies overnight to form areas of dense fog, especially near and north of the Missouri River in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog reduced visibility to one quarter of a mile intermittently at the Scobey airport mesonet site from the night on the 10th through the early morning on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 9th and 10th, a passing low pressure system moving southeast of the Pacific Northwest provided enough lift within an already moisture rich environment to produce widespread precipitation. Additionally, easterly upslope flow enhanced precipitation amounts, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front and Central Montana mountain areas. Snow levels were highly variable with this system, rising and falling with the diurnal cycle, but generally starting out above 7000 ft before eventually falling to around 3000 ft.  Areas near the Rocky Mountain Front including Marias Pass, Browning, and Rogers Pass observed  much of the winter weather impact, while eastern Pondera and Teton Counties received the heaviest rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 8 inches of snow accumulation within 24 hours at 3 W Dupuyer.  Message relayed via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous mid-level disturbance and attendant surface cold front moved through Montana  in a northwest to southeast direction on Sunday April 28th. This brought a several hour period of strong gusty northerly winds and moderate to heavy snow, which combined to produce blizzard conditions across much of North-central Montana. During the peak of the storm, wind gusts as high as 70 mph were reported, causing isolated power outages, motor vehicle accidents and weather related closures on area roadways, and a boating incident possibly attributed to gusty winds. The timing of the cold front passage possibly enhanced the impact of this storm because most of the event occurred before daytime heating was able to warm roadway surface temperatures above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind damage. Fairfield Sun Times reported a roof ripped off of a mobile home and trees down in the Fairfield area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 9th and 10th, a passing low pressure system moving southeast of the Pacific Northwest provided enough lift within an already moisture rich environment to produce widespread precipitation. Additionally, easterly upslope flow enhanced precipitation amounts, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front and Central Montana mountain areas. Snow levels were highly variable with this system, rising and falling with the diurnal cycle, but generally starting out above 7000 ft before eventually falling to around 3000 ft.  Areas near the Rocky Mountain Front including Marias Pass, Browning, and Rogers Pass observed  much of the winter weather impact, while eastern Pondera and Teton Counties received the heaviest rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a heavy snow accumulation of 12 inches at 7 SSE Neihart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the coldest arctic intrusions brought severe driving conditions due to poor visibility from blowing and drifting snow to portions of western Montana. Though total snowfall amounts from this event were marginal, the antecedent transportable snow already on the ground contributed to much of the blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Because of sub-zero ambient temperatures and wind chill temperatures, Blacktail Ski Resort closed on March 2. Snow amounts ranged from 2 inches in Eureka to 5.3 inches in Marion. The Boorman RAWS weather sensor near Marion saw wind gusts between 20 and 30 mph at times Friday evening March 1 through early morning Saturday which caused blowing and drifting snow with low visibility along US-2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were reported for MT-83 between Condon and Ferndale due to blowing and drifting snow. While only a trace of snow feel between West Glacier and Marias Pass, bitterly cold temperatures followed the arctic cold front passage in those areas. Other snow amounts ranged from 4.5 inches near Swan Lake to 8 inches at the Hungry Horse Dam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Travel Only, was issued for Lake County due to blowing and drifting snow with low visibility. Numerous accidents and slide-offs caused tow service to be backed up as much as three hours during the event in the Flathead Valley. While new snowfall ranged from two to four inches with up to 5.8 inches in Whitefish, the main cause of the high impact was more due to the combination of existing one to two feet of snow on the ground with strong winds sustained between 20 and 33 mph with gusts over 40 mph. The GPI airport recorded a peak wind speed of 43 mph at 7:41 pm on March 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance causing mid-level height falls, combined with effective deep-layer shear of around 40 kts and increasing MLCAPE (1000-1500 J/kg) resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms producing large hail and damaging winds.
A slow moving thunderstorm produced 3.70 inches of rain in a short period of time which resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Missouri River had 3 to 4 feet of water covering it. Water covered much of the bulldozers that were putting in a pipeline. In addition, a temporary bridge was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest arctic air mass of the winter brought 20 to 40 below zero temperatures across western Montana. Besides school delays, cars and buses not starting, a few cases of newborn cattle freezing to death were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USFS Eureka Ranger Station COOP recorded 23 below zero which made it the coldest temperature there since 1961. An unofficial temperature of 40 below was reported in Marion and 34 below at MacGregor Lake. The Yaak COOP reported 30 below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Air temperatures ranged from near 20 below in St. Ignatius and Kalispell to an unofficial 40 below zero in Lone Pine. A rancher in Lone Pine lost two calves due to the cold. While Ronan school buses were delayed, the Charlo buses did not run because of the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous mid-level disturbance and attendant surface cold front moved through Montana  in a northwest to southeast direction on Sunday April 28th. This brought a several hour period of strong gusty northerly winds and moderate to heavy snow, which combined to produce blizzard conditions across much of North-central Montana. During the peak of the storm, wind gusts as high as 70 mph were reported, causing isolated power outages, motor vehicle accidents and weather related closures on area roadways, and a boating incident possibly attributed to gusty winds. The timing of the cold front passage possibly enhanced the impact of this storm because most of the event occurred before daytime heating was able to warm roadway surface temperatures above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coop observer at station SHSM8 11 ESE Highwood reported 29 inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued melting of the remaining snowpack prolonged flooding concerns through the Milk River and Poplar River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuing the flooding from the end of March, recent melting of the larger snowpack allowed the Poplar river gauge reading (PLRM8) along the Poplar River to remain above the flood stage of 13.0 feet for a few more days into April. The river dropped back down below flood stage at 10:14 PM MST on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent arctic air mass combined with breezy westerly winds to produce wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower for most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With no observation sites available in northern Phillips County, the Val Marie Southeast Saskatchewan ASOS site was used as a proxy. Temperatures in the -10s and -20s combined with wind speeds up to 17 mph to create wind chills values in the -40s.  The coldest wind chill reading of -45 was recorded at 2:00 AM MST on the 3rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog reduced visibility to one quarter of a mile for about 2 1/2 overnight hours from the 10th into the 11th at the Glasgow airport ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the coldest arctic intrusions brought severe driving conditions due to poor visibility from blowing and drifting snow to portions of western Montana. Though total snowfall amounts from this event were marginal, the antecedent transportable snow already on the ground contributed to much of the blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 9 pm on March 1, the Missoula County sheriff department issued a MEANS 911 alert for Emergency Travel Only from Interstate 90 at Hellgate Canyon to Revenna just west of Bearmouth. They stated that there were multiple accidents due to white-out conditions. Between 20 to 30 inches of snow were already on the ground before the strong arctic winds pushed through causing the blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the coldest arctic intrusions brought severe driving conditions due to poor visibility from blowing and drifting snow to portions of western Montana. Though total snowfall amounts from this event were marginal, the antecedent transportable snow already on the ground contributed to much of the blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though minimal new snowfall amounts only ranged from a trace up to 1 inch, pre-existing transportable snow on the ground combined with strong wind gusts in constricted areas such as eastern Thompson Falls, generated blowing and drifting snow for a period. The Thompson Falls airport had a peak wind of 42 mph around midnight March 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the coldest arctic intrusions brought severe driving conditions due to poor visibility from blowing and drifting snow to portions of western Montana. Though total snowfall amounts from this event were marginal, the antecedent transportable snow already on the ground contributed to much of the blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor visibility due to heavy snow bands was followed by blowing and drifting snow caused by strong arctic easterly winds. At 9 pm, a MEANS alert was issued by the Missoula County sheriff's office to tell the public about poor driving conditions. Around midnight, Severe Driving was declared by the Montana Department of Transportation between Lolo and Missoula due to poor visibility from blowing snow and drifting on US-93. Snow drifts, approximately several feet in depth, were reported from the Rattlesnake, Missoula and Lolo locations. While new snowfall amounts between a half an inch at the Missoula Airport up to 5 inches in Lolo were observed, the antecedent transportable snow on the ground contributed to most of the low visibility and heavy drifting during this event. By the evening of March 2, wind chill temperatures ranged between 10 and 20 below zero.

During the late evening and early morning hours of the 18th and 19th a severe thunderstorm moved across portions of Powder River and Carter Counties. This thunderstorm produced damaging straight line winds in Powder River County and an EF-1 tornado in Carter County.

The coldest arctic air mass of the winter brought 20 to 40 below zero temperatures across western Montana. Besides school delays, cars and buses not starting, a few cases of newborn cattle freezing to death were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Potomac COOP recorded 37 below zero. A temperature of 30 below was reported at both the Seeley Lake RAWS and Clearwater Junction RAWS with an unofficial temperature of 32 below near Condon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous mid-level disturbance and attendant surface cold front moved through Montana  in a northwest to southeast direction on Sunday April 28th. This brought a several hour period of strong gusty northerly winds and moderate to heavy snow, which combined to produce blizzard conditions across much of North-central Montana. During the peak of the storm, wind gusts as high as 70 mph were reported, causing isolated power outages, motor vehicle accidents and weather related closures on area roadways, and a boating incident possibly attributed to gusty winds. The timing of the cold front passage possibly enhanced the impact of this storm because most of the event occurred before daytime heating was able to warm roadway surface temperatures above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported at the Hotel/Fairfield AWOS. Visibility was a quarter mile or less, with winds gusts of 47-52 mph.

Spring season snow melt combined with additional rainfall and ice jams along area rivers, to rise the level of many creeks and rivers above flood stage across northeast Montana through the last several days of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of March 23rd, at the Saco forecast point (SACM8) along the Milk River, recent snow melt and a possible ice jams caused the river level to quickly rise above the flood stage of 20.0 feet at 4:35 AM MST. Between multiple ice jams, the river level dropped back down below flood stage for just a few hours the next day before rising above flood stage again.  The river crested at 21.97 feet at 1:54 PM MST on the 25th. As the ice jam gradually cleared, the river fell back down below flood stage at 11:00 PM MST on the 27th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed in the Orchard Homes area of Missoula as the Clark Fork River rose above minor flood stage beginning midday on the 14th. The Clark Fork River's flow peaked at 9.15 feet late on the 18th, and finally fell below flood stage late on the 21st with residential flooding coming to an end.  Observed impacts included flooding on the north end of Tower Street and on Kehrwald Drive with some water into basements of adjacent homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized, steady to heavy snow bands affected portions of north-central Montana between the evening of February 15th and early morning of February 16th. As an eastward-moving shortwave trough traversed the region, a low pressure center aloft developed within this shortwave. The low's cyclonic circulation drew relatively-warm air northward and then around the backside of the low pressure center. This warm air encountered colder air, resulting in frontogenesis in the low- to mid-level atmosphere. The combination of lift induced by this frontogenesis and abundant low- to mid-level moisture contributed to the development of steady to heavy snow bands, generally north through west of the low pressure center aloft. The greatest snow totals were focused in Cascade County, where reports of about 6 to 12-inches of snow were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fresh snowfall of 8.0-inches since 11 PM MST February 15th. Snow fell at a rate of up to 2-inches per hour. Was still snowing at time of report. Location: 7-miles SW of Highwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture interacted with an intense arctic cold front producing widespread heavy snowfall across areas of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged snowfall and blustery northerly winds caused significant impacts to the Bitterroot and Missoula Valleys. Widespread accumulations of up to two feet of snowfall was observed in the Bitterroot valley. The City of Hamilton declared a snow emergency February 25, urging drivers to stay off roadways until conditions improve. Areas of snow and blowing snow in the southern part of the Missoula valley and northern sections of the Bitterroot valley led to a period of severe driving conditions and numerous accidents were reported. Many schools in the Bitterroot valley were closed for Tuesday, including Stevensville, Hamilton, and Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple shortwave troughs from the Pacific traversed north-central and southwest Montana during February 24th through 26th. Isentropic lift ahead of each shortwave resulted in periods of snow. A surface front near the southwest Montana/Idaho border separated Arctic air to the north from milder air to the south. The front wavered northward and southward through the period. This front served as an additional focus for snow, especially on February 26th, when bands of heavy snow occurred along and just north of the front over southwest Montana. These snow bands then exited southwest Montana to the south and east by the evening of the 26th as the front did the same.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT closed Targhee Pass during snowstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of February 4th, an Arctic high pressure ridge was present over Montana, especially east of the Continental Divide. A tight surface pressure gradient along the western flank of this ridge yielded breezy winds at MacDonald Pass. The combination of these winds and frigid air led to extreme wind chills at the pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MacDonald Pass MT DOT station reported wind chill of -49F with sustained wind of 17 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the height of the winter storm, winds gusted to 50 mph in the Missoula Valley, particularly in East Missoula, with visibilities of less than a quarter mile due to snow and blowing snow.  Minor power outages occurred and a few signs were damaged around Missoula. Travel was also severely impacted with numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass combined with plentiful Pacific moisture to spread accumulating snow through portions of Montana - especially south of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 14 S Winnett reported 8 inches of new snowfall in 24 hours.  Times estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass combined with plentiful Pacific moisture to spread accumulating snow through portions of Montana - especially south of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter at the Zortman Mine in SW Phillips County estimated 7 to 8 inches of new snowfall in 24 hours.  Times estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very cold and persistent arctic air mass over the region combined with winds from the west to bring wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower to many locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -10s and wind speeds from 18 to 25 mph, wind chill values dropped into the -40s for about 2 to 3 hours during the early morning on the 27th at the Medicine Lake RAWS site.  The coldest wind chill reading of -43 degrees was recorded at 4:58 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very cold and persistent arctic air mass over the region combined with winds from the west to bring wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower to many locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site, with temperatures in the -10s and wind speeds of 17 to 26 mph, wind chill values dropped into the -40s for about 1 1/2 hours in the morning of the 27th.  Similarly colder wind chills also occurred earlier, for less than one hour during the midnight hour.  The coldest wind chill reading of -43 was recorded from 12:01 to 12:11 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air once again spilled over the Divide bringing snow, high winds, blowing and drifting snow, along with low wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow and high winds brought severe driving conditions and significant drifting of snow. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were common with wind gusts of 40 to 50 miles per hour with Glacier Park International Airport peaking at 51 miles per hour late Friday evening. Considerable drifting of snow led to several stranded vehicles and to the closure of some west to east roads in the Flathead Valley, especially the north end of the valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy conditions were common on area roads as freezing rain proceeded an arctic blast. Once gusty winds arrived, falling and blowing snow combined to produce near zero visibility at times on Interstate 90, making travel nearly impossible.  Snowfall amounts of up to 12 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain proceeded the blast of arctic air and accompanying snowfall.  Winds gusting from 40 to 50 mph were common in the Missoula Valley, accompanied by moderate to heavy snowfall. General snowfall amounts were 2 to 5 inches with higher amounts to 7 inches in the southern Bitterroot Valley. Minor power outages also occurred because of the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Similar to other areas, freezing rain caused slick travel in the Flathead and Mission Valleys overnight  before the arctic cold front blasted through the area. Winds with the front peaked at 54 mph at the Glacier Park International airport. Besides temperatures  falling into the teens, the strong winds contributed to multiple downed large trees, and severe blowing and drifting snow.  The drifting prompted the Flathead County Sheriff to close Hodgson Road later Sunday. Six foot snow drifts were reported outside of Ronan in the Mud Lake area.  Many schools from Polson to Arlee were preemptively delayed or closed for Monday classes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very cold and persistent arctic air mass over the region combined with winds from the west to bring wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower to many locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -10s and winds speeds of 16 to 31 mph, wind chill values in the -40s persisted during the early morning hours on the 27th at the Sioux Pass MT-16 DOT site.  The coldest wind chill reading of -42 degrees was recorded at 3:40 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very cold and persistent arctic air mass over the region combined with winds from the west to bring wind chill values of 40 below zero and lower to many locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the -10s and -20s and wind speeds of 9 to 15 mph, wind chill values dropped into the -40s in the early morning on the 27th at the Malta airport ASOS site.  The coldest wind chill reading of -46 degrees was recorded both at 2:35 AM and 4:35 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture overran an arctic air mass across northwest Montana leading to significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall led to multiple avalanches on I-90 near Lookout Pass. Westbound lanes of I-90 were closed at St. Regis through the morning of the 14th as crews cleared several feet of snow off the roadway. A passenger vehicle was partially buried by the avalanche and the driver had to be rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture interacted with an intense arctic cold front producing widespread heavy snowfall across areas of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy prolonged snowfall impacted travel over Lolo and Lost Trail passes where one to two feet of snowfall was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture interacted with an intense arctic cold front producing widespread heavy snowfall across areas of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall and blowing snow led to areas of severe driving conditions and closure of schools, including Butte and Anaconda. According to Butte school district officials, the school closed on February 16 for the first time since 1989. Widespread areas of 9 to 13 inches of snow was observed in Silver Bow county. Up to 30 inches of snow was reported in Anaconda! Numerous accidents were reported on stretches of I-90 near Anaconda and Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The event started out with freezing rain along US-2 between West Glacier and Marias Pass Saturday night. Strong winds, estimated to 50 mph by an NWS employee vacationing near Essex, ushered in sub-zero temperatures and wind chills between 20 and 40 below zero.  A local ski resort had to close three of their chairs and cancel an outdoor event because of the high winds and cold. This region saw anywhere from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall on top of the freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Before the bitterly cold arctic winds and snow began, temperatures rose into the 40s and 50s.  The arctic air rushed westward causing temperatures to drop and moisture to freeze on roadways, particularly impacting US-93. The strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow especially north of Whitefish on US-93 on the 3rd. Snow amounts ranged from 3 inches in Libby up to 8 inches along Highway 56 near Bull Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep arctic air mass combined with overrunning moisture made for a high impact event for much of western Montana with heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow, wind damage, mixed precipitation, and slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds came over the Divide ushering in sub-zero temperatures and wind chills between 25 and 40 below zero. The highest wind gust observed was 63 miles per hour at Helmville.  This combined with falling and blowing snow to produce visibilities below a quarter mile at times and treacherous travel conditions. General snowfall across the region ranged between 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between late evening of February 8th and late morning of the 9th, an Arctic cold front moved from near the I-90 corridor in western and southwest Montana to a position extending southeastward from northwestern Idaho to east-central Idaho. Behind this front, a strong Arctic high pressure ridge settled southward across north-central and southwest Montana. This ridge was accompanied by a tight surface pressure gradient, especially over southwest Montana and along the Rocky Mountain Front. The tight pressure gradient resulted in breezy surface winds. In turn, the combination of these winds and frigid temperatures yielded warning-criteria wind chills for parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citizen Weather Observer Program (CWOP) station recorded wind chill of -41F accompanied by sustained wind of 25 mph. Location: 2-miles NNW of Joplin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air continued to spill over the Divide with extremely low wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 10 to 20 miles per hour combined with sub-zero temperatures brought wind chills of 25 to 35 degrees below zero. The coldest wind chill was 53 below zero on the morning of the 9th at MacDonald Pass located near 6300 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air once again spilled over the Divide bringing snow, high winds, blowing and drifting snow, along with low wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds and widespread snow brought significant drifting of snow and severe driving conditions. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were common with wind gusts of 20 to 30 miles per hour. Considerable drifting of snow caused great difficulty for snow plows working on U.S. Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-positioned snow storm, in a stalled weather pattern lingered over northeast Montana for a few days, allowing for 8 to 16 inches of new snow accumulation for many eastern Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer at 2 E Wibaux measured 8 inches of new snowfall within 24 hours.  Times estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-positioned snow storm, in a stalled weather pattern lingered over northeast Montana for a few days, allowing for 8 to 16 inches of new snow accumulation for many eastern Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of 12 to 16 inches of new snowfall were common across Prairie County.  Times estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-positioned snow storm, in a stalled weather pattern lingered over northeast Montana for a few days, allowing for 8 to 16 inches of new snow accumulation for many eastern Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer estimated 11 inches of new snowfall within 24 hours.  MT DOT road conditions web-site showed Dawson County in severe driving conditions.  Times estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged cold spell led to an ice jam along the Madison River, near Ennis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Madison County Disaster and Emergency Services reported ice jam-related flooding near the US-287 bridge just outside downtown Ennis. Minor property damage occurred along the river. Exact location and duration of flooding unknown. Exact location of damage unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a prolonged cold spell continued, a renewed ice jam and associated flooding affected the Ennis area. In addition, a separate ice jam and related flooding developed along the Gallatin River, near Logan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Madison County Disaster and Emergency Services reported renewed flooding from an ice jam along the Madison River. Floodwater was flowing across the US-287 bridge just east-southeast of downtown Ennis. Exact duration of flooding unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic front crossed north-central and southwest Montana on February 2nd and 3rd. The sharp surface trough along this front interacted with a surface high pressure ridge to the south. The resulting tight mean sea-level pressure gradient permitted a high wind gust in the White Sulphur Springs area during the morning of the 2nd. The bigger impacts stemmed from periods of snow, heavy at times, that developed behind the Arctic front as multiple Pacific shortwave troughs traversed the area on the 2nd into the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White Sulphur Springs Airport AWOS recorded 58-mph peak gust. AWOS located 4-miles south of White Sulphur Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trained spotter from Malta to Saco measured or estimated 6 to 7 inches of new snow fall.

High winds also led to a few downed trees and brief power outages. Heavy snowfall of 6 to 12 inches accumulated by the end of the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads froze up with ice as freezing rain proceeded arctic air and snowfall. Although snowfall amounts only ranged from 3 to 7 inches, the icy road conditions followed by snowfall made for treacherous travel over the mountain passes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-zero temperatures and 10 to 20 mph winds combined to produce wind chill values of -30 to -40 for lower elevations on US Highway 2. Extreme values to -63 were observed at the Snow Slip observing site, located near 7000 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-zero temperatures and 10 to 20 mph winds combined to produce wind chill values of -25 to -35 for lower elevations. Extreme values to -49 were observed at the MacDonald Pass observing site, located near 6300 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air continued to spill over the Divide with extremely low wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub-zero temperatures combined with winds of 10 to 20 miles per hour brought wind chills of 30 to 40 degrees below zero in the lower elevations along US-2.  The coldest lower elevation wind chill was 39 below zero at Essex on the morning of the 10th with 64 below zero at Snow Slip located near 7000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and expansive winter storm from the Pacific Northwest and the Canadian Rockies followed an exact track through northeast Montana, bringing up to a foot of snow to some locations in less than 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wibaux County Dispatch reported 7 inches of new snow fall. I-94 was closed through Wibaux County from 19:20 MST on the 3rd to 06:00 MST on the 4th due to severe winter driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture overran an arctic air mass across northwest Montana leading to significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall with rates of 2 inches per hour were common. Two to three feet of snowfall was observed in Libby and Troy and school delays and closures were reported for February 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture overran an arctic air mass across northwest Montana leading to significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted travel over Marias Pass on Highway 2, with two feet of snowfall being reported. Isolated areas of blowing snow also led to significantly reduced visibility at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture overran an arctic air mass across northwest Montana leading to significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of heavy snowfall was observed from the evening of the 11th through the afternoon of the 13th. Lookout Pass Ski Resort reported 28 inches of snowfall in a 24 hour period on the morning of the 13th. 39 inches of snowfall was reported in Heron. School delays and closures also were in affect for the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low over Southeast Alberta/Southwest Saskatchewan slides east northeastward across Southern Canada, all the while dragging a cold front across North Central Montana. Showers and thunderstorms develop ahead of the advancing cold front, most notably in a area of moderate instability and 0-6km bulk shear of 20-30kts over the plains of North Central Montana. This instability and shear support a few strong to severe thunderstorms, primarily along and northeast of a St Mary, to Choteau, to White Sulphur Springs. Northeast of this line, forecast includes winds approaching 60 mph and hail approaching quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size or larger hail measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex upper level low with several circulation centers located over western Canada spreads southeastward into the northern Rockies and Montana. An embedded wave moving into southern AB drags a cold front at the surface eastward across the region. This, while an unseasonably strong upper level jet noses eastward across southern ID. Showers and thunderstorms concentrate over northern portions of the forecast area in association with the wave moving into southern AB, as well as along areas near the WY/ID border in association with the upper level jet and surface cold front hanging up in that area. Forecast includes isolated strong to severe thunderstorms in these areas during the early evening hours with sufficient shear/instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several branches down in Havre. Damage and branch sizes are estimated. One was approximately 8 to 10 inches in diameter, but it came from a dead tree. The other one was from a live tree that was possibly 2 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface dewpoints begin in the 40s and low 50s; however, a low level easterly flow advects ample moisture into the region. At the same time, surface based cape values |and mid-level lapse rates increase to 1000 to 1500 J/kg and 7 to 8 degrees C/km, respectively. These factors combined with good wind shear result in a severe thunderstorm watch issued for much of the CWA, valid until 10 PM MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail measured by trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms develop east of the Southern Rocky Mountain Front and move northeastward across the area. These storms move into a moist environment with good jet dynamics, wind shear, and instability. As a result, a severe thunderstorm watch is issued for most of the CWA. The primary thunderstorm threats are severe hail of equal to or greater than quarter size and wind gusts of up to 60 to 80 mph. Heavy downpours of rain are also expected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Second hand report of quarter size hail reported in Clancy by KRTV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly to northwesterly flow aloft and embedded shortwave disturbances affect the CWA. Simultaneously, temperatures remain near-normal as high pressure aloft builds slowly from the west. Isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms remain possible, especially during the afternoon and evening hours, as well as over and near higher terrain. No severe thunderstorms are anticipated, since instability and vertical shear |should remain modest. Nevertheless, strong wind gusts, small hail, and downpours may accompany any storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received multiple reports and pictures from spotters, 911 dispatch and the public of at least one funnel cloud that moved over the Bozeman area, persisting for 15 to 20 minutes. Based upon pictures shared, this was most likely a cold air funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary closed low located over southern Saskatchewan generates areas of rain over northeastern Montana, including portions of Blaine County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of road closure through Mission Canyon due to high water and some culvert damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: H500 ridge flattens throughout the day, with numerous and subtle mid- to upper level waves rippling through the nearly zonal flow. Models discrepancies continue with the exact timing, placement, and strength of these waves through the early evening |hours. These discrepancies lead to overall low confidence forecast with respect to location/lack thereof of the highest PoPs across Southwest and North Central Montana. With respect to precipitation timing, while there is not a consensus in the exact timing of the synoptic forcing arriving overhead, diurnal heating plays a huge role in the shower/thunderstorm development, especially initially over higher terrain. Best window for precipitation, 13 Jun/18z through 14 Jun/03z (plus or minus 3 hours on either end). With the loss of diurnal heating, precipitation coverage and intensity diminishes around 14 Jun/06z. While 0-6km bulk shear is less than ideal (15-25kts) |for the maintenance/(development) of (severe) thunderstorms, a marginally unstable atmosphere supports a few stronger thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours, especially over/in close proximity to the mountains. Due to increasing mid-level cloudiness, there is only moderate confidence in hitting high temperature forecast. Regardless, highs exceed climatological norms for the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea-size to quarter-size hail cover the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Storm Prediction Center includes a portion of the CWA in a marginal risk area for severe Thunderstorms. A mid-level shortwave trough dives southeastward from British Columbia and Alberta. This trough works with a relatively moisture rich environment producing showers and thunderstorms by early afternoon. This area roughly extends north and east of a Cut Bank to Lewistown line. A few long-lived severe thunderstorms form in an environment containing moderate instability and favorable shear. The storms track east southeastward along and just behind an advancing surface front. Strong storms form along this frontal boundary where daytime heating provides elevated CAPE. With outflow dominance, storms merge into more of a storm cluster as they move eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 1.25 inch hail with 0.4 inches rain. Estimated wind gusts 40 to 50 mph.

This jet streak provided for strong upper level divergence. |At the same time, a strong upper level disturbance moved over the forecast area. |All the above, combined with low-level moisture and wind shear, resulted in severe thunderstorms across portions of the Billings Forecast area.

Favorable winds, plentiful surface moisture, and a very unstable atmosphere all supported the development of many severe thunderstorms, and sustained them through most the evening across much of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several power poles blown down along seven-mile road, between US Highway 191 and Zortman.  Trees were bending in the wind as the storm passed. Power was out across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough approached and eventually moved through North-central and Southwest Montana on Saturday, August 10th and the morning of Sunday, August 11th. A belt of southwesterly mid- to upper-level winds up to about 50 knots preceded the axis of this trough and contributed to moderate to strong vertical wind shear, which favored the development of organized thunderstorms in the form of multicells and supercells. At the surface, winds exhibited an easterly component at times. These surface winds transported greater low-level moisture into the area from the Dakotas. Farther aloft, southwesterly winds near 10,000 feet MSL were present ahead of the Pacific trough axis and transported abundant amounts of Pacific moisture into the region. The resulting unusually-high humidity and daytime heating contributed to a weak to moderate instability especially over Southwest Montana. Also notable was a pronounced elevated mixed layer (EML), a term for an elevated dry layer of the lower to middle atmosphere, moving north from Utah and eastern Idaho. EMLs are much more common across the Great Plains of the United States and are associated with many of the classic spring severe weather outbreaks in the central part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported hail estimated to be the size of quarters (1 inch Diameter) near Park Lane. hail was covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 2nd, 2019, a disturbance embedded within a southwesterly flow provided wind shear and additional moisture to an unstable environment.  At the surface, a weak Pacific cold front traversed the region, lending further lift for thunderstorm development. The lower atmosphere remained on the dry side, increasing the threat from gusty and erratic thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KGTF ASOS measured a 62 mph (54 kts) thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms collided together just south of Flathead Lake and produced a severe gust front that tracked north to the Flathead Valley. Multiple observers reported that the strong winds lasted for up to 15 minutes. Dozens of toppled trees, damaged power poles, transmission and distribution lines were reported from Bigfork up to Columbia Falls from strong winds. Power outages lasted for 24 hours. The golf course in Bigfork reported losing 59 trees, mostly spruce from 20 to 30 feet tall with diameters between 18 inches and 2.5 feet. The restaurant at the golf course lost all their food due to the power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain, combined with snow melt from the northern Big Horn Mountains, resulted in areas of flooding across portions of Yellowstone, Big Horn and Powder River Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the area, combined with snow melt over the Big Horn Mountains, resulted in rising water levels along the Powder River in Powder River County. Although there were reports of numerous flooded fields, no significant impacts were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain, combined with snow melt from the northern Big Horn Mountains, resulted in areas of flooding across portions of Yellowstone, Big Horn and Powder River Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that the Big Horn River flowed at bank full or just out of its banks from Two Leggins Bridge into the Hardin vicinity. Heavy rain and snow melt from the northern Big Horn Mountains from the 24th through the 28th resulted in water rises on a number of creeks, streams and rivers which feed into the Big Horn River. This was also compounded by a flash flood event on the 27th that sent around a 10,000 cfs surge of water down the Big Horn River. Some flooding of rural fields was reported. Otherwise,  no major impacts were reported.

Other roads that were affected by flood waters included Fly Creek Road and Lower Canal Road. Some of this flooding was due to very heavy rain from thunderstorms upstream during the evening of the 26th.

A belt of southwesterly mid- to upper-level winds up to about 50 knots preceded the axis of this trough and contributed to moderate to strong vertical wind shear, which favored the development of organized thunderstorms in the form of multicells and supercells. At the surface, winds exhibited an easterly component at times. These surface winds transported greater low-level moisture into the area from the Dakotas. Farther aloft, southwesterly winds near 10,000 feet MSL were present ahead of the Pacific trough axis and transported abundant amounts of Pacific moisture into the region. The resulting unusually-high humidity and daytime heating contributed to a weak to moderate instability especially over Southwest Montana. Also notable was a pronounced elevated mixed layer (EML), a term for an elevated dry layer of the lower to middle atmosphere, moving north from Utah and eastern Idaho. EMLs are much more common across the Great Plains of the United States and are associated with many of the classic spring severe weather outbreaks in the central part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail size of 1 inch in diameter. Also, hail cracked a windshield on a side by side and stripped most of the leaves from an aspen grove. the hail started as sporadic large stones but then smaller stones fell for over 20 minutes.

On August 25th, 2019, a moist and modestly unstable west to northwester flow aloft provided conditions for shower and thunderstorm potential. A subtle shortwave trough then moved into North-central Montana that afternoon, bringing this potential to reality. Low LCL heights along with modest low level shear likely factored in the development a funnel cloud over Joplin Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter took a photo of a cold air funnel near Joplin. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 19th, weak instability combined with a subtle disturbance and attendant cold front to provide sufficient dynamics for isolated thunderstorm development across Central and Southwest Montana. One thunderstorm near Forest Grove in Fergus County produced quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter stated that the hail size started out as marble sized but then increased to the size of quarters (1 inch diameter). Hail was covering the ground at the time of the report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm was situated over portions of western Big Horn County during the late evening hours on the 26th into the early morning hours of the 27th. Several inches of rain fell across this area within just a few hours. Flash flooding occurred on Pryor, Fly and Woody Creeks which resulted in damage to roads and bridges. Culverts were also displaced. Damage to trap and skeet field buildings at a fishing and shooting club was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: H500 shortwave trough exiting the Pacific Northwest begins to move into the Northern Rockies as of the afternoon, with the strongest height falls overspreading the region. At the surface, 992mb low slowly slides east across East Central Alberta and into West Central Saskatchewan, with a weak surface high building east from the Pacific Northwest towards the Northern Rockies. The resultant pressure gradient along the Continental Divide combined with mechanical mixing along an eastward advancing  Pacific cold front leads to breezy west to southwest winds across much of North Central and Southwest Montana during the afternoon hours. These breezy surface winds relax somewhat across Southwest and Central Montana with the loss of mixing, but remain breezy along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the plains of North Central Montana. Wind speeds increase further along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the adjacent plains/foothills west of the Interstate-15 corridor after the midnight hour as |H700 cross barrier flow increases from 35-45kts up to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 58 mph measured at mesonet station jpkm8 Judith Peak RAWS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports wind gust of 60 mph 9 W Fort Belknap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Trough approached from the west and supplied sufficient wind shear, lift, moisture, and instability for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms over southwest Montana. Effective shear values fell in the 30 to 40 knot range, while there was about .75 inches of precipitable water in the area. The first wave of storms developed by early afternoon and produced wind gusts up to 80 mph in Gallatin County. Thunderstorms continued to develop in the late afternoon and evening hours, which produced quarter sized hail in Jefferson and Beaverhead Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports quarter sized hail falling and covering the ground near Cardwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm over portions of southeast Montana produced large hail and some damaging winds.

A ridge of high pressure was flattened by an approaching trough of low pressure situated over British Columbia and eastern Alberta. A strong westerly cross barrier flow then developed over the Rocky Mountain Front. Winds in the upper levels were unseasonably strong, with 300 mb wind speeds around 80 kts. This resulted in surface winds approaching and in some cases exceeding 60 mph over north-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was reported at the Great Falls WFO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level closed low and associated trough began to dig southeast along the British Columbia Coast during the morning hours of Friday, September 27th, 2019, reaching over Vancouver Island during the evening hours on Friday.  By Saturday (September 28, 2019) morning the trough had dug substantially along the Pacific Coast of the United States, with the broad closed low positioned over the western two-thirds of the state of Washington and northern third of Oregon. As the day progressed on Saturday the trough dug even further south into Southern California and began to take on a neutral tilt, all the while the closed low slowly meandered to over the Oregon and Washington borders. This neutrally tilted trough would then persist over the western third of the United States through Sunday (September 29, 2019) evening, with the closed low remaining nearly stationary over the Columbia River Valley of Oregon and Washington over this time frame. This upper level pattern from Friday through Monday (September 30, 2019) allowed for abundant Pacific moisture to be transported east and northeast over the Northern Rockies, which combined with strong easterly and northeasterly upslope flow in the low-to-mid levels and cold air filtering south from Arctic, allowed for widespread snow to develop over North Central and portions of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT reports severe driving conditions on Montana highway 200 from Rogers Pass to the highway 434 Junction.

Wind speeds increase further along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the adjacent plains/foothills west of the Interstate-15 corridor after the midnight hour as |H700 cross barrier flow increases from 35-45kts up to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust of 63 mph measured at mesonet station mtbrn 1.3 E East Glacier Park (mt dot).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level closed low and associated trough began to dig southeast along the British Columbia Coast during the morning hours of Friday, September 27th, 2019, reaching over Vancouver Island during the evening hours on Friday.  By Saturday (September 28, 2019) morning the trough had dug substantially along the Pacific Coast of the United States, with the broad closed low positioned over the western two-thirds of the state of Washington and northern third of Oregon. As the day progressed on Saturday the trough dug even further south into Southern California and began to take on a neutral tilt, all the while the closed low slowly meandered to over the Oregon and Washington borders. This neutrally tilted trough would then persist over the western third of the United States through Sunday (September 29, 2019) evening, with the closed low remaining nearly stationary over the Columbia River Valley of Oregon and Washington over this time frame. This upper level pattern from Friday through Monday (September 30, 2019) allowed for abundant Pacific moisture to be transported east and northeast over the Northern Rockies, which combined with strong easterly and northeasterly upslope flow in the low-to-mid levels and cold air filtering south from Arctic, allowed for widespread snow to develop over North Central and portions of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Choteau Public Works Director requested that people stay off the roads due to fallen trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaches the area. 3hr pressure rises in wake of this front exceed 5mb over the plains of North Central Montana as a 1040mb surface high (over West |Central Alberta/East Central British Columbia) slides south southeast along the Continental Divide and towards the Northern Rockies. Winds switch to the north/northeast behind the frontal passage and are sustained at around 15-25kts, with |gusts approaching 35kts. These winds combined with temperatures in the teens lead to wind chill values as low as the single digits below zero, with these values only expected to fall further as the ambient air temperatures falls into the single digits below zero to single digits above zero. Snow is heavy at times with and briefly following the cold front. Very strong surface and mid-level frontogenetic forcing is expected. Additionally, northerly surface winds aid in ideal upslope conditions for northern facing slopes and the adjacent foothills, with heaviest snow falling at/near the mid-slopes. As the surface high and positively tilted H500 trough slides/pivots over the region, expect precipitation to decrease in areal coverage from north to south, with the one exception being in |those northern facing upslope regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports 8 inches of snow on the ground with visibility reduced in blowing and drifting snow 11 SSE Ulm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: H500 shortwave trough exiting the Pacific Northwest begins to move into the Northern Rockies as of the afternoon, with the strongest height falls overspreading the region. At the surface, 992mb low slowly slides east across East Central Alberta and into West Central Saskatchewan, with a weak surface high building east from the Pacific Northwest towards the Northern Rockies. The resultant pressure gradient along the Continental Divide combined with mechanical mixing along an eastward advancing  Pacific cold front leads to breezy west to southwest winds across much of North Central and Southwest Montana during the afternoon hours. These breezy surface winds relax somewhat across Southwest and Central Montana with the loss of mixing, but remain breezy along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the plains of North Central Montana. Wind speeds increase further along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the adjacent plains/foothills west of the Interstate-15 corridor after the midnight hour as |H700 cross barrier flow increases from 35-45kts up to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Average 3 hr wind speed of 42 mph measured from 3 MST to 6 MST 14 W Bynum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwesterly flow ahead of a trough of low pressure situated over British Columbia transported abundant mid and upper level moisture into the region, while the lower levels remained dry. This combined with an unstable atmosphere created the conditions for isolated thunderstorms with a potential for strong to severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported that a portion of a corrugated metal roof was blown off, with debris hitting a transformer and causing a power outage. The event time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale trough draped over the western 1/3rd of the US, with the axis |generally orientated northeast to southwest from Western Montana.  At the surface, a weak area of low pressure developed east of the Continental Divide over Central |Montana during the afternoon, while a cold front over Alberta quickly dropped south along the eastern slopes of the Rockies. Precipitable water values ranged from around .5 to .75 inches and there was a modest amount of cape and shear present. Showers and thunderstorm activity generally remained confined to the areas of best synoptic forcing over Central and Southwest Montana. One storm near the community of Buffalo produced heavy rain that resulted in flooding of the Buffalo Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwater was flowing across property on West Buffalo Creek. The Floodwaters were up to four feet deep and the floodwater channel was about 100 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow aloft, combined with a disturbance aloft, resulted in heavy snow across the Beartooth/Absaroka Foothills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chains were required on both Homestake and MacDonald Passes as early as 9:45 pm mdt (MacDonald Pass). There were snowfall reports of 10 south of Butte, 3-4 at Anaconda, with estimates of 13-14 on Homestake Pass. Winds observed on ASOS up to 30 mph were common. The Eastbound Lane of I-90 was completely blocked by multiple stuck semi-trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous plume of moisture pushed over northwest Montana, while a cold front pushed westward from east of the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:26 am mdt on the 9th reports of snowfall amounts and hazardous driving prompted issuance of an advisory. Charlo Heights reported 6 of snowfall, while other locations in the southern Bitterrroot reported 2 to 5. Multiple slide-offs were reported in Hamilton  by the emergency manager of a federal facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well advertised cold front moves across the western Hi-Line area. Gusty westerly winds ahead of the front remain below warning criteria, resulting in the cancellation of the High Wind Warning for the Northern Rocky Mountain front and adjacent plains. Front continues dropping southward, reaching the Great Falls area between 8 and 9 AM, then moving into the Helena area around 11 AM to noon, and into the Bozeman area by late afternoon. Gusty west winds to shift more northerly along with quick drop in temperatures, with frontal passage. Most areas across the north and central observe high temperatures in the morning, with some near normal daytime highs in the southwest. Widespread precipitation expected lagging the front by a couple of hours for some areas. Precipitation begins as rain for a period before switching over to snow. Then, widespread snow Tuesday night through the day Wednesday. Best chances for snow along the Rocky Mountain Front and then from Great Falls to Lewistown and southward. North of this line brief period of snow and little accumulations. The remainder of moderate to brief heavy snow at times with blowing snow in gusty winds. Snow then diminishes later in the day through the evening Wednesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimates 5 inches of snow 13 W Cut Bank so far this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level closed low and associated trough began to dig southeast along the British Columbia Coast during the morning hours of Friday, September 27th, 2019, reaching over Vancouver Island during the evening hours on Friday.  By Saturday (September 28, 2019) morning the trough had dug substantially along the Pacific Coast of the United States, with the broad closed low positioned over the western two-thirds of the state of Washington and northern third of Oregon. As the day progressed on Saturday the trough dug even further south into Southern California and began to take on a neutral tilt, all the while the closed low slowly meandered to over the Oregon and Washington borders. This neutrally tilted trough would then persist over the western third of the United States through Sunday (September 29, 2019) evening, with the closed low remaining nearly stationary over the Columbia River Valley of Oregon and Washington over this time frame. This upper level pattern from Friday through Monday (September 30, 2019) allowed for abundant Pacific moisture to be transported east and northeast over the Northern Rockies, which combined with strong easterly and northeasterly upslope flow in the low-to-mid levels and cold air filtering south from Arctic, allowed for widespread snow to develop over North Central and portions of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Great Falls (5 WSW Great Falls) reported 19.3 inches for a storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Tuesday, September 24th, 2919, a surface low over Southeastern Saskatchewan continued to move away from the region, which allowed a tight pressure gradient on the lee side of the Rockies to slowly relax.   However, a Pacific disturbance approached from the west and began to introduce strong cross barrier flow, essentially continuing and even briefly strengthening the surface winds over the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains. This resulted in non-thunderstorm winds exceeding High Wind Criteria for a short period of time at the Cut Bank Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank Airport ASOS (2 SW Cut Bank) reported a Non-thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph (51kt).
