EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breaks in the cloud cover allows the atmosphere to de-stabilize across portions of the region, which combined with a relatively moist northernly flow leads to the development of scattered to numerous showers. These showers combined with continued upslope flow along north facing slopes leads to periods of moderate snow across portions of Central and Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began at 9 pm MST on 25 Oct. Snow was measured at 10:34 am MST. 6 inches of snowfall fell within 13.5 hours.

This neutrally tilted trough would then persist over the western third of the United States through Sunday (September 29, 2019) evening, with the closed low remaining nearly stationary over the Columbia River Valley of Oregon and Washington over this time frame. This upper level pattern from Friday through Monday (September 30, 2019) allowed for abundant Pacific moisture to be transported east and northeast over the Northern Rockies, which combined with strong easterly and northeasterly upslope flow in the low-to-mid levels and cold air filtering south from Arctic, allowed for widespread snow to develop over North Central and portions of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blackfeet Nation Tribal council reports that all roads in and out of the Blackfeet Nation Reservation have been closed or are experiencing severe driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Trough approached from the west and supplied sufficient wind shear, lift, moisture, and instability for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms over southwest Montana. Effective shear values fell in the 30 to 40 knot range, while there was about .75 inches of precipitable water in the area. The first wave of storms developed by early afternoon and produced wind gusts up to 80 mph in Gallatin County. Thunderstorms continued to develop in the late afternoon and evening hours, which produced quarter sized hail in Jefferson and Beaverhead Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported about two dozen large trees downed by thunderstorm winds at the KOA near West Yellowstone. Some tents and awnings were destroyed. Wind speed and time were estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well advertised cold front moves across the western Hi-Line area. Gusty westerly winds ahead of the front remain below warning criteria, resulting in the cancellation of the High Wind Warning for the Northern Rocky Mountain front and adjacent plains. Front continues dropping southward, reaching the Great Falls area between 8 and 9 AM, then moving into the Helena area around 11 AM to noon, and into the Bozeman area by late afternoon. Gusty west winds to shift more northerly along with quick drop in temperatures, with frontal passage. Most areas across the north and central observe high temperatures in the morning, with some near normal daytime highs in the southwest. Widespread precipitation expected lagging the front by a couple of hours for some areas. Precipitation begins as rain for a period before switching over to snow. Then, widespread snow Tuesday night through the day Wednesday. Best chances for snow along the Rocky Mountain Front and then from Great Falls to Lewistown and southward. North of this line brief period of snow and little accumulations. The remainder of moderate to brief heavy snow at times with blowing snow in gusty winds. Snow then diminishes later in the day through the evening Wednesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured 8 inches of snow in 6 hours. 0.81 inches of liquid reported for this snow event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: H500 shortwave trough exiting the Pacific Northwest begins to move into the Northern Rockies as of the afternoon, with the strongest height falls overspreading the region. At the surface, 992mb low slowly slides east across East Central Alberta and into West Central Saskatchewan, with a weak surface high building east from the Pacific Northwest towards the Northern Rockies. The resultant pressure gradient along the Continental Divide combined with mechanical mixing along an eastward advancing  Pacific cold front leads to breezy west to southwest winds across much of North Central and Southwest Montana during the afternoon hours. These breezy surface winds relax somewhat across Southwest and Central Montana with the loss of mixing, but remain breezy along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the plains of North Central Montana. Wind speeds increase further along the Rocky Mountain Front and over the adjacent plains/foothills west of the Interstate-15 corridor after the midnight hour as |H700 cross barrier flow increases from 35-45kts up to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 50 kts / 58 mph measured at mesonet station mtbrn 1.3 E. East Glacier Park (MT DOT).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow aloft becomes westerly as a strong upper level trough develops over western Canada. Surface low pressure gradients along the Rocky Mountain Front tighten as low pressure develops in Alberta and mountain top level winds increase to between 50 and 70 knots. High Wind Watch upgraded to a Warning for the Northern Rocky Mountain Front and the western Hi-Line Counties. Short term models and the National Blend of Models (NBM) suggest winds in this region verify the warning by way of several hours of |sustained winds greater than 40 mph rather than by strong wind gusts as is normally the case.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds measured greater than 50 mph for 2 consecutive hours at 8 SE East Glacier Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system continued to feed a relatively moist southwest flow into the region.  The dynamic weren't quite conducive for severe weather but there was enough moisture and instability for the development of showers and thunderstorms with the capability to produce localized flooding due to heavy rains.  Southern Judith Basin County was one area that observed this type of thunderstorm activity, which resulted in localized flooding in Eddies Corner vicinity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimate of 1 to 2 inches of rain, with flood water reaching up to the height of the seal rim on truck tires.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed early in the afternoon across North-Central Montana as a shortwave trough moved through the area. There was moderate instability and adequate shear to support a few strong to severe thunderstorms, especially over the eastern Counties of the CWA. The Danvers area in Fergus County suffered the most significant impacts from these storms, with golf ball sized hail and crop damage being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball sized hail and crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A of broad low pressure area over the Pacific Northwest exerted an influence on Montana's weather for much of the weekend. This resulted in a persistent unstable southwest flow over the region, which pumped in mid and upper level moisture along with passing disturbances embedded within the flow.  In the upper levels, a jet stream  was situated in a favorable position to produce upper level divergence.  Meanwhile, a low level easterly flow was present for a significant period of time, which further increased moisture levels and wind shear. During the peak of this event, precipitable water values approached the one inch mark, effective wind shear values were as high as 50kts, and mid-level lapse rates soared to 7.5 to 8 degree C per Kilometer. The combination of these conditions provided an environment primed for a weekend of severe thunderstorms for much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports a few half dollar sized hail that occurred with an estimated 60 mph wind gust. A horse trailer was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north to northwesterly flow continues aloft as a shortwave moves southeast and exits the CWA. A tightening gradient along The Rocky Mountain Front and Central Montana mountains continues to support gusty winds through Tuesday morning. Winds over The Northern Rocky Mountain Front and Eastern Glacier County approach High Wind Warning criteria into Tuesday morning. High Wind Warning set to begin at 0Z Monday. Also, Winter Weather Advisories issued to address the blowing/drifting snow over The Rocky Mountain Front, Judith Basin, Eastern Cascade, Glacier and Pondera Counties remain unchanged. Areas where the snow hasn't compacted and gusty winds are present, see some reduced visibilities and snow drifting onto roads, causing some impacts to travel. During the day on Tuesday, the pressure gradient relaxes somewhat but winds aloft keep the breezy conditions going for much of the remaining week. Most areas are expected to be dry but areas along the immediate eastern slopes of The Northern Rocky Mountain Front are forecast to see some light snow overnight Monday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station delmt dellwo (mcscn).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Montana during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 5th. Rainfall anywhere from 1 to 6 inches was reported across this area. In addition, large hail also occurred with a few of these thunderstorms. The rain and hail combined resulted in flash flooding across some areas. One location had a few basements flooded, but otherwise, besides water over roadways, no major impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream, combined with a passing low pressure disturbance, just enough to mix down and spread strong and gusty winds to southwestern portions of Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, sustained winds of at least 40 mph were recorded each hour from the afternoon of the 22nd to just after midnight of the 23rd.  Peak wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded both at 9:45 PM and 11:45 PM MDT on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream, combined with a passing low pressure disturbance, just enough to mix down and spread strong and gusty winds to southwestern portions of Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station at the Zortman Mine, 1 W Zortman, recorded a 58 mph wind gust between 11:32 PM on the 22nd, and 12:32 AM on the 23rd.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream, combined with a passing low pressure disturbance, just enough to mix down and spread strong and gusty winds to southwestern portions of Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the BLM Mesonet site, 9.5 E Zortman, a 58 mph wind gust was first recorded at 10:00 PM MST on the 22nd. Severe wind gusts continued for almost 2 hours, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph recorded at 12:00 AM MST on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening and early morning hours of the 18th and 19th a severe thunderstorm moved across portions of Powder River and Carter Counties. This thunderstorm produced damaging straight line winds in Powder River County and an EF-1 tornado in Carter County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early morning of July 19th at 12:44am, a tornado briefly touched down 3 miles northeast of Ridgeway in Carter County. The tornado was 100 yards wide and was on the ground for a half a mile. There was considerable damage to buildings on a local ranch. A shop building had 2 doors blown in and roof damage. Two other sheds had significant roof damage. Several metal rooftops were torn off and peeled back. A small garage was destroyed with a vehicle displaced inside, turned and moved by a couple of feet. In addition, several trees were snapped (approximately 1 foot diameter), with other smaller trees blown over or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle weather disturbance moved through southern Saskatchewan, and combined with a hot and humid air mass to form a few severe thunderstorms which eventually crossed the border into far northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Lake RAWS site recorded a 69 mph wind gust. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching low pressure system from the west and unstable atmosphere combined over Montana to produce severe thunderstorms which eventually clustered together and moved through eastern Montana with very strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 68 mph wind gust was measured at the South Sawmill Creek RAWS site (SSMM8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching low pressure system from the west and unstable atmosphere combined over Montana to produce severe thunderstorms which eventually clustered together and moved through eastern Montana with very strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 74 mph wind gust was measured at the Lufborough Hill MT-200 DOT site (MTLUF).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant weather pattern across the region with a hot and humid air mass favored the development of severe thunderstorms which easily pushed a severe gust front from west to east across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 mph wind gust was recorded at the Saint Marie Mesonet MARCO site (07MT).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A of broad low pressure area over the Pacific Northwest exerted an influence on Montana's weather for much of the weekend. This resulted in a persistent unstable southwest flow over the region, which pumped in mid and upper level moisture along with passing disturbances embedded within the flow.  In the upper levels, a jet stream  was situated in a favorable position to produce upper level divergence.  Meanwhile, a low level easterly flow was present for a significant period of time, which further increased moisture levels and wind shear. During the peak of this event, precipitable water values approached the one inch mark, effective wind shear values were as high as 50kts, and mid-level lapse rates soared to 7.5 to 8 degree C per Kilometer. The combination of these conditions provided an environment primed for a weekend of severe thunderstorms for much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch sized hail reported near Lewistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest wind shear combined with a highly unstable atmosphere to produce thunderstorms with hail and severe wind gusts over Hill County. Cape values approached 2000 j/kg despite the area being under the influence of mid-level ridging. High DCAPE values up to 1700 j/kg was a key factor that pushed thunderstorm wind gusts to severe limits that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1.6 inches of rain in less than 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest wind shear combined with a highly unstable atmosphere to produce thunderstorms with hail and severe wind gusts over Hill County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into western Montana causing rapid freeze on the wet roads, wind, brief heavy snow, and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple slide offs (reported by MT DOT) prompted County officials to urge the public to travel only if necessary at 3:30 pm mdt. Highway 271 was closed due to semi-trailers blocking roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into western Montana causing rapid freeze on the wet roads, wind, brief heavy snow, and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road conditions rapidly changed as wet roads quickly became snow covered and icy. Car accidents numbered 22 by noon on the 28th according to DOT road reports. An NWS volunteer reported large pine trees down on MT-35, visibilities dropping from 7 miles to a 1/4 with wind, and their car being pushed around on road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into western Montana causing rapid freeze on the wet roads, wind, brief heavy snow, and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to numerous road accidents, Granite County Sheriff urged the public to drive only as necessary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into western Montana causing rapid freeze on the wet roads, wind, brief heavy snow, and poor visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a rapid freeze up of roads, blowing snow, and low visibilities, the Missoula Police Department sent an alert requesting essential driving only.  Approximately an hour later, Missoula County Emergency Management issued a MEANS (Missoula Emergency Activation Notification System) Alert for Emergency Travel Only both directions on Interstate 90 through Missoula County.|An NWS Missoula employee witnessed school buses having difficulty driving without slipping while driving at a speed of less than 5 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous plume of moisture pushed over northwest Montana, while a cold front pushed westward from east of the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind shift from the east, beginning at 8 am the 8th, signaled passage of the frontal boundary. Snow rates of greater than 1 per hour were experienced for 3 to 4 hours. Widespread snow amounts of 3 to 4 were observed, while Marias Pass had an estimated total (from water equivalent measured by HADS site) of 12. The DOT roads report described driving conditions with 'high winds' and a tree down at 8am, on the 9th. A private citizen tweet described driving conditions as limited vis, heavy snow, and tough driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous plume of moisture pushed over northwest Montana, while a cold front pushed westward from east of the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts to 40 mph were observed for multiple hours at the Narrows bouy weather station, and gusts to 30 mph were measured at the Yellow Bay Hill weather station. Mission Power reported 13 people without power at 7am on the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous plume of moisture pushed over northwest Montana, while a cold front pushed westward from east of the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of power outages in the St Regis area, and a reported 640 people were without power in the Alberton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass pushed a strong cold front through Montana, which triggered accumulating snowfall for many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow reports in the Zortman area of southwest Phillips County, ranged from 5 to 9 inches, with the largest report being 9.2 inches from the cooperative observer in Zortman.  Times estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass pushed a strong cold front through Montana, which triggered accumulating snowfall for many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 7 inches of snow were common across southern Petroleum County, including this public report at 6 WSW Flatwillow, relayed through Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass pushed a strong cold front through Montana, which triggered accumulating snowfall for many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although lighter snow amounts of 4 to 5 inches were common over eastern and central portions of Garfield County, a public report of 7 inches at 2 E Mosby, in southwestern Garfield County, was relayed through Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stronger winds aloft, were able to impact locations just downwind of higher elevations near central and eastern Montana, due to favorable atmospheric conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, a 59 mph wind gust was first recorded at 4:00 PM MST.  The peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at 4:10 PM.  After 4:15 PM, the severe wind gusts had subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Montana during the afternoon and early evening hours of the 5th. Rainfall anywhere from 1 to 6 inches was reported across this area. In addition, large hail also occurred with a few of these thunderstorms. The rain and hail combined resulted in flash flooding across some areas. One location had a few basements flooded, but otherwise, besides water over roadways, no major impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain was reported 6 miles west/northwest of Powderville. Corral Creek was well out of its banks with water flowing over roadways.

Rainfall anywhere from 1 to 6 inches was reported across this area. In addition, large hail also occurred with a few of these thunderstorms. The rain and hail combined resulted in flash flooding across some areas. One location had a few basements flooded, but otherwise, besides water over roadways, no major impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported flowing over several roads. A report indicated 4 inches of rain fell within 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the west, with many smaller-scale disturbances combined with a very unstable atmosphere, to allow several strong to severe thunderstorms to develop and move eastward through northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public estimated 60 mph wind gusts.

A surface trough built in from the north from the Great Basin into the Northern Rockies, while southeasterly winds at the surface brought in low level moisture into the region. Pacific moisture increased in the upper levels as an approaching trough. This combination lead to scattered showers and thunderstorms to development over the western and southwest mountains before moving out onto the plains that afternoon. Some thunderstorms produced large hail and one complex of storms with heavy rain and strong winds moved over the Great Falls area resulting reports of wind damage and urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Meagher County DES reported quarter to half dollar sized hail.

It was also reported that the underpass by the police station had cars submersed in flood water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system from the west focused widespread heavy rain showers and thunderstorms across most of the region.  The prolonged heavy rain caused some locations to experience flash flooding and areal flooding, before the storms diminished on the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rain, accumulating to at least 4.75 inches. Many roads in the area were closed under multiple feet of water. One particular low water crossing had approximately 10 feet of water rushing over it.  Flooding end time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plentiful surface moisture from the southeast and strong winds aloft from the northwest combined with a very unstable atmosphere, to produce some rare late-morning strong thunderstorms, a few of which became severe across far eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported golf ball size hail, along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed off the higher terrain of southwestern Montana, then moved into the more favorable atmosphere over eastern Montana to strengthen to severe levels through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported damage to a grain bin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed off the higher terrain of southwestern Montana, then moved into the more favorable atmosphere over eastern Montana to strengthen to severe levels through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed off the higher terrain of southwestern Montana, then moved into the more favorable atmosphere over eastern Montana to strengthen to severe levels through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed off the higher terrain of southwestern Montana, then moved into the more favorable atmosphere over eastern Montana to strengthen to severe levels through the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 75 mph wind gust was recorded at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated thunderstorms produced some large hail. The largest hail was reported in Sheridan County Wyoming.
A couple of isolated thunderstorms produced some large hail. The largest hail was reported in Sheridan County Wyoming.
A couple of isolated thunderstorms produced some large hail. The largest hail was reported in Sheridan County Wyoming.
An upper level disturbance within a southwest flow aloft combined with a strong jet streak resulted in numerous severe thunderstorms across the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed at Missouri and Fourth St in Miles City. The tree fell on and smashed a fence. Multiple branches with diameters of 3 to 4 inches were down in Riverside Park. In addition, a tree fell on and knocked a street light off a power pole on the 2100 block of Main Street. Other trees and branches with diameters of 6 inches were down across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening and early morning hours of the 18th and 19th a severe thunderstorm moved across portions of Powder River and Carter Counties. This thunderstorm produced damaging straight line winds in Powder River County and an EF-1 tornado in Carter County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several round hay bales were moved 200 yards. In addition, several tree limbs of 4 to 5 inches in diameter were downed several feet from the tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air and converging surface winds allowed thunderstorms to easily form along a subtle boundary, then suddenly grow severe as they moved toward the east and southeast into an even more favorable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prairie County DES Coordinator reported water over I-94 near Terry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching Pacific trough provided many of the necessary ingredients for severe weather including moisture, wind shear, upper level jet dynamics, and elevated instability. However, a copious amount of cloud cover during the peak heating of the day prevented the true severe potential from materializing. Regardless, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over the western and southwest mountains before moving out onto the plains that afternoon. Most of these storms stayed below severe limits with the exception of a storm that produced 1 inch hail Beaverhead County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail sized one inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching trough of low pressure combined with an unstable atmosphere to form several individual strong thunderstorms, which combined roughly into a large severe thunderstorm cluster, and moved through eastern Montana through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported intermittent hail, eventually up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure approached from the northwest, while embedded disturbances within the subsequent southwest flow lifted northeast across the area. At the surface, a stalled out front was draped over southwestern Montana and was beginning to slowly retreat to the north as a warm front. Diurnal heating combined with the moist southwest flow aloft, and low level northerly/easterly flow aided in the development of isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms over north-central Montana. One thunderstorm that developed over the Garneill area in Fergus County was strong enough to produce a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud spotted Garneill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast low-level surface flow advected very moist air from the central plains westward across southeast and south-central Montana. Dewpoints were in the 50s to lower 60s across the Billings Forecast Area. At the same time,  a very strong jet max moved across central and south central Montana. In addition to the above, lapse rates were steep across the area along with high CAPE values. Strong speed and directional shear was in place over the Billings forecast area resulting in organized, rotating updrafts. As a result, storms across the area produced very large hail and damaging winds.||Significant damage was done to Shepherd schools. There was roof damage and extensive damage to the bus barn. In addition, all the west side windows were broken out from combined hail and wind.||Storm damage also occurred to a few commercial planes at the Billings Airport. Frontier and Alaska Airlines both had planes damaged by the hailstorm. In addition, fallen trees and branches knocked out power to about 1,500 residents and emergency crews responded to a car wreck in the Heights with reports of people trapped in an elevator. |The storm also destroyed one-third of the barley and wheat crop, approximately $400,000 worth,  at a farm in Molt. ||In addition, The storm killed and maimed 11,000 to 13,000 waterfowl and wetland birds at the Big Lake Wildlife Management Area west of Molt. Montana Fish, Wildlife & Parks biologists picked up dead ducks and shorebirds with broken wings, smashed skulls, and internal damage and other injuries consistent with massive blunt-force trauma. They reported thousands of additional dead or badly injured waterfowl and wetland birds in and around the lake. An estimated 20 to 30 percent of the birds at the lake were killed or injured.

A Pacific trough approached and eventually moved through North-central and Southwest Montana on Saturday, August 10th and the morning of Sunday, August 11th. A belt of southwesterly mid- to upper-level winds up to about 50 knots preceded the axis of this trough and contributed to moderate to strong vertical wind shear, which favored the development of organized thunderstorms in the form of multicells and supercells. At the surface, winds exhibited an easterly component at times. These surface winds transported greater low-level moisture into the area from the Dakotas. Farther aloft, southwesterly winds near 10,000 feet MSL were present ahead of the Pacific trough axis and transported abundant amounts of Pacific moisture into the region. The resulting unusually-high humidity and daytime heating contributed to a weak to moderate instability especially over Southwest Montana. Also notable was a pronounced elevated mixed layer (EML), a term for an elevated dry layer of the lower to middle atmosphere, moving north from Utah and eastern Idaho. EMLs are much more common across the Great Plains of the United States and are associated with many of the classic spring severe weather outbreaks in the central part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Great Falls Fire department reported a structure fire caused by lightning striking a tree and adjacent house. The department received the report shortly after 10 AM. The timing of the lightning strike was determined by the lightning detection network. Also, the property damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast low-level surface flow advected very moist air from the central plains westward across southeast and south-central Montana. Dewpoints were in the 50s to lower 60s across the Billings Forecast Area. At the same time,  a very strong jet max moved across central and south central Montana. In addition to the above, lapse rates were steep across the area along with high CAPE values. Strong speed and directional shear was in place over the Billings forecast area resulting in organized, rotating updrafts. As a result, storms across the area produced very large hail and damaging winds.||Significant damage was done to Shepherd schools. There was roof damage and extensive damage to the bus barn. In addition, all the west side windows were broken out from combined hail and wind.||Storm damage also occurred to a few commercial planes at the Billings Airport. Frontier and Alaska Airlines both had planes damaged by the hailstorm. In addition, fallen trees and branches knocked out power to about 1,500 residents and emergency crews responded to a car wreck in the Heights with reports of people trapped in an elevator. |The storm also destroyed one-third of the barley and wheat crop, approximately $400,000 worth,  at a farm in Molt. ||In addition, The storm killed and maimed 11,000 to 13,000 waterfowl and wetland birds at the Big Lake Wildlife Management Area west of Molt. Montana Fish, Wildlife & Parks biologists picked up dead ducks and shorebirds with broken wings, smashed skulls, and internal damage and other injuries consistent with massive blunt-force trauma. They reported thousands of additional dead or badly injured waterfowl and wetland birds in and around the lake. An estimated 20 to 30 percent of the birds at the lake were killed or injured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a car port blown across a street where it damaged a pickup truck. vehicles moved and damaged by wind gusts. Penny size hail was also reported with damage to some crops.  Wind gust speed estimated. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast low-level surface flow advected very moist air from the central plains westward across southeast and south-central Montana. A Pacific trough approached and eventually moved through North-central and Southwest Montana on Saturday, August 10th and the morning of Sunday, August 11th. A belt of southwesterly mid- to upper-level winds up to about 50 knots preceded the axis of this trough and contributed to moderate to strong vertical wind shear, which favored the development of organized thunderstorms in the form of multicells and supercells. At the surface, winds exhibited an easterly component at times. These surface winds transported greater low-level moisture into the area from the Dakotas. Farther aloft, southwesterly winds near 10,000 feet MSL were present ahead of the Pacific trough axis and transported abundant amounts of Pacific moisture into the region. The resulting unusually-high humidity and daytime heating contributed to a weak to moderate instability especially over Southwest Montana. Also notable was a pronounced elevated mixed layer (EML), a term for an elevated dry layer of the lower to middle atmosphere, moving north from Utah and eastern Idaho. EMLs are much more common across the Great Plains of the United States and are associated with many of the classic spring severe weather outbreaks in the central part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public posted an image of hail sized 1.25 inches in diameter to KXLF.

On august 21st, an upper level trough over the Pacific Northwest ushered a moist and unstable southwest flow into the region, while much of North-central Montana was situated on the northeast side of a stationary front, where unusually strong easterly surface winds pumped in low level moisture from the plains. Limited cloud cover helped propel mid-level lapse rates to the 8 to 8.5 degree C.  As a Pacific cold front associated with the aforementioned trough approached from the west, thunderstorms developed along the southern Rocky Mountain Front and then moved northeastward onto the plains. These storms produced hail, strong gusty thunderstorm winds, and brief but heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer reported quarter sized (1 inch in diameter) hail.

In addition, fallen trees and branches knocked out power to about 1,500 residents and emergency crews responded to a car wreck in the Heights with reports of people trapped in an elevator. |The storm also destroyed one-third of the barley and wheat crop, approximately $400,000 worth,  at a farm in Molt. ||In addition, The storm killed and maimed 11,000 to 13,000 waterfowl and wetland birds at the Big Lake Wildlife Management Area west of Molt. Montana Fish, Wildlife & Parks biologists picked up dead ducks and shorebirds with broken wings, smashed skulls, and internal damage and other injuries consistent with massive blunt-force trauma. They reported thousands of additional dead or badly injured waterfowl and wetland birds in and around the lake. An estimated 20 to 30 percent of the birds at the lake were killed or injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind and hail blew out several windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On august 21st, an upper level trough over the Pacific Northwest ushered a moist and unstable southwest flow into the region, while much of North-central Montana was situated on the northeast side of a stationary front, where unusually strong easterly surface winds pumped in low level moisture from the plains. Limited cloud cover helped propel mid-level lapse rates to the 8 to 8.5 degree C.  As a Pacific cold front associated with the aforementioned trough approached from the west, thunderstorms developed along the southern Rocky Mountain Front and then moved northeastward onto the plains. These storms produced hail, strong gusty thunderstorm winds, and brief but heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was still over the curb on the northwest side of the intersection of 3rd St NW approximately a half hour after the rain had ended. The outer outbound lane 3rd St NW was closed by the fire department and public works. Estimated water depth was 4 to 7 inches over the closed lanes, with 2 to 4 inches over the inner lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast low-level surface flow advected very moist air from the central plains westward across southeast and south-central Montana. Dewpoints were in the 50s to lower 60s across the Billings Forecast Area. At the same time,  a very strong jet max moved across central and south central Montana. In addition to the above, lapse rates were steep across the area along with high CAPE values. Strong speed and directional shear was in place over the Billings forecast area resulting in organized, rotating updrafts. As a result, storms across the area produced very large hail and damaging winds.||Significant damage was done to Shepherd schools. There was roof damage and extensive damage to the bus barn. In addition, all the west side windows were broken out from combined hail and wind.||Storm damage also occurred to a few commercial planes at the Billings Airport. Frontier and Alaska Airlines both had planes damaged by the hailstorm. In addition, fallen trees and branches knocked out power to about 1,500 residents and emergency crews responded to a car wreck in the Heights with reports of people trapped in an elevator. |The storm also destroyed one-third of the barley and wheat crop, approximately $400,000 worth,  at a farm in Molt. ||In addition, The storm killed and maimed 11,000 to 13,000 waterfowl and wetland birds at the Big Lake Wildlife Management Area west of Molt. Montana Fish, Wildlife & Parks biologists picked up dead ducks and shorebirds with broken wings, smashed skulls, and internal damage and other injuries consistent with massive blunt-force trauma. They reported thousands of additional dead or badly injured waterfowl and wetland birds in and around the lake. An estimated 20 to 30 percent of the birds at the lake were killed or injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft continues to yield mild and windy conditions across much of the forecast area through Sunday. Occasional light snow, rain, and some mixed precipitation continues for the Northern Rockies through Saturday night, before transitioning to mostly snow thereafter. The most impactful weather remains above pass level. Colder air moves in aloft and snow accumulation becomes more efficient. High Wind Warnings for the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of North-central Montana expire at midnight but the windy conditions continue into Saturday. Although, the flow aloft will remain strong, warning criteria winds are  not forecast at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbrn two medicine bridge (mt dot).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable weather pattern steered a jet stream across the region.  Atmospheric conditions allowed most of those winds to reach down to the surface downslope of the Little Rocky Mountains of southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site MTMAL, sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph were recorded each hour through most of the morning.  Peak wind gusts of 57 mph were recorded from 930 AM to 935 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An otherwise low-impact winter storm from the Canadian Rockies suddenly strengthened as it passed over northern Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 10 NE Whitewater estimated 8 inches of new snowfall, while another trained spotter in the town of Whitewater estimated 7 inches of new snowfall and some snow drifts up to 4 feet high.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures lead to snow melt and runoff across the region, but most notably over the plains of North Central Montana. With the ground still frozen, ponding of melt water noted in low lying areas. Additional runoff of this melt water into creeks and streams increase the potential for ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports and photos from Fergus County disaster and Emergency Services Coordinator and National Weather Service Hydrologist. Roadways washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds along the Rocky Mountain Front increase...become strong and gusty, then spreading out over the adjacent plains, with areas of blowing and drifting snow. Strong downslope pressure gradients fuels winds...strongest gusts, as high as 75 mph, confined to the immediate Rocky Mountain Front. Gusts up to 65 mph in the Cut Bank area, especially overnight. Time-height profiles show 60 knot winds just off the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbrn, two medicine bridge mdt measured 59 kt / 68 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures lead to snow melt and runoff across the region, but most notably over the plains of North Central Montana. With the ground still frozen, ponding of melt water noted in low lying areas. Additional runoff of this melt water into creeks and streams increase the potential for ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water came up really fast yesterday in the late afternoon. Slowed somewhat during the night but expecting increases again this afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures provided by Outdoor Recreation Planner for Upper Missouri River Breaks NM, Fort Benton, Fergus County. James Kipp boat ramp had to be closed and locked due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north to northwesterly flow continues aloft as a shortwave moves southeast and exits the CWA. A tightening gradient along The Rocky Mountain Front and Central Montana mountains continues to support gusty winds through Tuesday morning. Winds over The Northern Rocky Mountain Front and Eastern Glacier County approach High Wind Warning criteria into Tuesday morning. High Wind Warning set to begin at 0Z Monday. Also, Winter Weather Advisories issued to address the blowing/drifting snow over The Rocky Mountain Front, Judith Basin, Eastern Cascade, Glacier and Pondera Counties remain unchanged. Areas where the snow hasn't compacted and gusty winds are present, see some reduced visibilities and snow drifting onto roads, causing some impacts to travel. During the day on Tuesday, the pressure gradient relaxes somewhat but winds aloft keep the breezy conditions going for much of the remaining week. Most areas are expected to be dry but areas along the immediate eastern slopes of The Northern Rocky Mountain Front are forecast to see some light snow overnight Monday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station shbm8, spring hill near browning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of disturbances separated by brief shortwave ridging provided a four day stretch of wet weather for much of the region. The first system moved into southwest Montana on Friday, September 6th, 2019 and brought a brief period of strong to severe thunderstorms to the southwest followed by heavy rains, with some expansion into central and the eastern portions of North-central Montana. The second system moved toward the area by Sunday September 8th, 2019 and featured more general moderate to heavy rains over the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of Central Montana. Precipitable water values exceeded the one inch mark at times and rarely fell below .7 inches throughout this event. At the surface, a frontal system became nearly stationary and became an impetus for precipitation. The severe weather in the southwest was mostly driven by instability along with lift from the stalled frontal system and the first disturbance, while the second was largely influenced by easterly upslope winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph (51 kt) thunderstorm wind gust was reported at the Beaverhead Deerlodge mesonet station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle shortwave trough moved toward the region on Friday, September 13th, 2019 and worked with a modestly unstable atmosphere provided by diurnal heating to produce scattered thunderstorms over Central and North-central Montana. The most significant impact from the storms was strong gusty winds and while almost all wind gusts remained below severe limits, a wind gust of 62 mph was observed at the Lewistown Airport in Fergus County. Strong westerly flow aloft was occurring while the storms were developing and may have contributed to the strong thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lewistown Airport ASOS (2 WSW Lewistown) reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph (54kt).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic snow and gusty winds brought considerable travel impacts due to slick, snow-covered roadways and reduced visibility. Traffic accidents were common on Interstate 90, especially west of Butte on Sunday morning with chains required on Homestake Pass for a time. Snow accumulations of 4 to 10 inches were common which also limited backcountry travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall of 1 to 2 inches fell in the Missoula and Bitterroot valleys with up to 6 inches on the south side of Hamilton. While impacts were limited, this was an unusually early snowfall for valley locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General snow amounts of 4 to 8 inches fell, locally up to 12 inches. Also, wind gusts to 35 mph occurred. Difficult travel was experienced by backcountry users with periodic slippery, snow-covered, primary roads such as US-93 at Lost Trail Pass and US-12 at Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 1 to 3 inches fell in the valleys with 5 to 10 inches in the higher elevations. Also, wind gusts to 40 mph were reported in Thompson Falls and on some of the higher terrain. Difficult travel was experienced by backcountry users with periodic slippery, snow-covered, primary roads such as Interstate 90 at Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches leading to difficult backcountry travel and occasional slick, snow-packed, primary roadways. Strong winds brought down numerous trees on local trails used by outfitters especially around Upper Holland Lake which significantly slowed packing operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm over Powder River County produced some sub-severe hail.
An isolated thunderstorm over Powder River County produced some sub-severe hail.

Gusty southwest winds continue across much of north-central MT through Tuesday with strongest winds and widespread blowing and drifting snow occurring along and just east of the Rocky Mountain Front. Aside from some mountain snow and freezing drizzle, mainly|along the Continental Divide, generally dry conditions prevail with temperatures trending above seasonal averages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station delmt. 15.2 SW Dupuyer (mcscn). 14 W Bynum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system that tapped subtropical moisture brought an extensive area of heavy rain to Northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General rainfall of 1 to 2 inches fell across parts of the Flathead and Mission valleys eastward to the Continental Divide. Heavier amounts of 3 to 4 inches fell locally on the east side of Flathead Lake and in the Mission Mountains, which were near record 2-day totals for the month of September. Also, minor debris flows were noted on Hwy 35 between Bigfork and Polson with some water into the Bigfork after-school building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds developed on the western side of the Whitefish and Mission ranges as high pressure settled into north-central Montana resulting in considerable damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage to tree and power lines was concentrated in and around Lake Blaine, Swan Lake, and the eastern shore of Flathead Lake. The strongest wind gust were estimated near 60 mph in these areas. Power outages began early Saturday and lasted into Sunday evening for some with thousands of customers impacted. Thirty trees and five power lines were reported down across Highway 35 which resulted in the road being closed to only local traffic. A conservative estimate of a couple hundred downed trees occurred in populated areas. On the west side of Flathead Lake, significant boat and dock damage occurred as waves reached over 6 feet. At the Dayton Yacht Harbor, 2 sailboats sank due to damage sustained from impacting a dock with another 5 boats destroyed. In addition, another dozen boats were damaged at the Somers Bay Marina.  Only intermittent cell phone service was available in Flathead and Lake counties due to cell phone towers damaged by falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds developed on the western side of the Whitefish and Mission ranges as high pressure settled into north-central Montana resulting in considerable damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees fell on power lines resulting in power outages, especially during the predawn hours. Damage was concentrated on the east side of Highway 93, particularly the Eureka neighborhoods of Glen Lake, Indian Creek Road, Graves Creek Road, and Burma Road. Also, tree and power line damage occurred in Stryker and Star Meadows. In addition, cell phone service was unavailable for all of Lincoln County Saturday afternoon and evening because of wind damage to cell towers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall was focused near the Continental Divide with 2 to 3 feet common.  Social media videos from near Marias Pass indicated up to 4 feet of snow fell along with drifting snow as winds gusted to 40 mph.  Further west in the Whitefish Range, general snowfall was 4 to 8 inches with wind gust to 50 mph at Big Mountain. Periods of hazardous travel were common on Highway 2, especially near Marias Pass due to the combination of snow, wind, and low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but compact storm system moving through eastern Montana combined with an unstable atmosphere to form strong thunderstorms - a few of which became severe through Wibaux, Dawson, and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Pine Hill RAWS site (PNHM8).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, but compact storm system moving through eastern Montana combined with an unstable atmosphere to form strong thunderstorms - a few of which became severe through Wibaux, Dawson, and Richland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dawson County Law Enforcement reported 2 inches of standing water on I-94, located 8 miles southwest of Glendive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass caused strong wind and damage to portions of northwest Montana, while southwest Montana experienced a flash-freeze with heavy snow bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power company reported trees on power lines with 14 separate power outages in the Flathead Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass caused strong wind and damage to portions of northwest Montana, while southwest Montana experienced a flash-freeze with heavy snow bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marias Pass received 4.9 inches of snow during this event. Roads became slick as temperatures fell below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low- to mid-level disturbance over northwestern WY and vicinity during the early afternoon of May 17th reached northwestern SD and vicinity by early afternoon of the 18th. At that time, the disturbance exhibited a well-defined cyclonic circulation center over northwestern SD. Isentropic lift accompanying this disturbance triggered periods of rain and mountain snow in north-central Montana on the 17th into the 18th. At lower elevations, rain mixed with or changed to wet snow on occasion during the late evening of the 17th through mid-morning of the 18th. This stemmed from unusually-cold air accompanying the system and the wet-bulb effect. Snowfall totals were generally no more than several inches with minimal impacts. However, significant and impactful snowfall occurred in vicinity of the Little Rockies in Blaine County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer reported 11.0-inches of storm-total snowfall and cars in the ditch while traveling through Lodge Pole. Post-event report. Exact duration of snowfall unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwest winds continue across much of north-central MT through Tuesday with strongest winds and widespread blowing and drifting snow occurring along and just east of the Rocky Mountain Front. Aside from some mountain snow and freezing drizzle, mainly|along the Continental Divide, generally dry conditions prevail with temperatures trending above seasonal averages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station ricmt 4.9. E Pendroy (mcscn). 5 ESE Pendroy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowstorm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to parts of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell in the valleys with 5 to 10 inches in the higher elevations, local amounts to 20 inches were observed at the Hawkins Lake SNOTEL. Also, wind gusts to 35 mph were reported in the higher terrain, locally over 50 mph at the western base of the Whitefish Range. Backcountry users experienced difficult travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of disturbances separated by brief shortwave ridging provided a four day stretch of wet weather for much of the region. The first system moved into southwest Montana on Friday, September 6th, 2019 and brought a brief period of strong to severe thunderstorms to the southwest followed by heavy rains, with some expansion into central and the eastern portions of North-central Montana. The second system moved toward the area by Sunday September 8th, 2019 and featured more general moderate to heavy rains over the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of Central Montana. Precipitable water values exceeded the one inch mark at times and rarely fell below .7 inches throughout this event. At the surface, a frontal system became nearly stationary and became an impetus for precipitation. The severe weather in the southwest was mostly driven by instability along with lift from the stalled frontal system and the first disturbance, while the second was largely influenced by easterly upslope winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 71 mph (62 kt) thunderstorm wind gust was reported the Brenner Raws mesonet station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching low pressure trough combined with a very unstable atmosphere to form a number of strong thunderstorms, one of which became severe as it moved through the northeast corner of Montana. KGGW WSR-88D was down for the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball to hen egg size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system, slowly moving through the northern Rockies region, combined with a cold Canadian air mass to spread record amounts of snowfall through most of central Montana, especially over the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Zortman measured 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft continues to yield mild and windy conditions across much of the forecast area through Sunday. Occasional light snow, rain, and some mixed precipitation continues for the Northern Rockies through Saturday night, before transitioning to mostly snow thereafter. The most impactful weather remains above pass level. Colder air moves in aloft and snow accumulation becomes more efficient. High Wind Warnings for the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of North-central Montana expire at midnight but the windy conditions continue into Saturday. Although, the flow aloft will remain strong, warning criteria winds are  not forecast at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbwg browning (mt dot).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture brought a wintry mix of precipitation across northwest Montana December 19th and December 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall quickly transitioned to freezing rain across lower elevation valley areas in the Lower Clark Fork region. A light glaze of ice accumulation caused slippery conditions on Highway 200 and I-90. Five inches of snow accumulated at Lookout Pass causing the Montana Department of Transportation to require chains on towing units over the pass during the evening of the 19th into the morning hours of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls caused dangerous travel conditions across portions of western Montana during the evening hours of December 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall lead to heavy snow with visibility well below 1/4 of a mile between Frenchtown and Missoula. The rapid change in visibility caused difficult driving conditions and accidents on I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls caused dangerous travel conditions across portions of western Montana during the evening hours of December 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall caused dangerous driving conditions on I-90 between East Missoula and Clinton. Heavy snow lead to visibility well below 1/4 of a mile. An overturned semi truck was reported near Clinton due to slick roads and poor visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures and snow melt provided moisture at the surface, which combined with clear skies and calm wind to produce periods of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glendive airport ASOS site KGDV, visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for nearly 7 hours from the night of the 22nd into the early morning of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridging from the Pacific NW to the Canadian prairies becomes suppressed as a potent upper level jet and accompanying plume of Pacific moisture moves onto the BC/WA coast. Jet and moist plume noses into the Northern Rockies late then orients more northwesterly and sags southward as a surface low emerges east of the Canadian Rockies and kicks out across the Prairies. Main hazards associated with this system are strong winds across portions of north-central MT and mountain snow along The Continental Divide affecting the forecast area. Winds increase Monday night through Tuesday morning along the east slopes as the surface pressure gradient increases across The Rockies with strong gusts possible by Tuesday morning along areas immediately east of The Rocky Mountain Front. By Tuesday night strong winds are likely to expand further east as strongest winds aloft and an embedded mid level shortwave moves across the region. Period of strongest winds looks to continue through at least Wednesday morning for these areas. With favorable temp/wind profiles for enhanced downslope winds expected east of The Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station shbm8, spring hill near browning (hads).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very tight north/south pressure gradients, combined with a strong jet stream overhead, resulted in powerful wind gusts across the Livingston area and adjacent foothills. The highest wind gust reported was 80 mph at the Livingston airport. Due to multiple semi roll-overs,  I-90 west bound between mile markers 330 and 337 was closed for a period of time. The high winds continued through the morning hours of January 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted 58 to 61 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate snowfall the evening of December 12th into the morning of December 13th led to winter driving conditions over Lookout Pass on I-90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snowfall during the evening of December 12th into the morning of December 13th lead to slippery conditions on I-90 over Lookout Pass. Several semi trucks spun out traveling over Lookout Pass, leading to the closure of the interstate at 1132 pm December 12th to approximately 430 am on December 13th. Only 5 inches of snow was reported at Lookout Pass Ski Area. Temperatures were in the low 30s at pass level, and the nearby Mullan Pass ASOS only briefly reported visibility down to 1/2 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls caused dangerous travel conditions across portions of western Montana during the evening hours of December 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted travel on I-90 between Superior and Ninemile with visibility well below 1/4 mile. Travel was significantly impacted over Evaro Hill on US 93. Several accidents caused travel to come to a stand still and lead to chains being required for towing units over Evaro Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed over the Bitterroot Mountains during the evening of December 15th, bringing impacts to portions of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively long lived snow squall caused dangerous driving conditions on US 93 between Missoula and Lolo. Visibility well below 1/4 mile was observed with snowfall rates of two inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most damaging northeast wind events in the past several decades occurred across western Montana. Wind speeds nearing 70 mph caused acres of downed trees and subsequent power and residential infrastructure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility remarked that this was one of the most damaging storms they've ever experienced. The owner of a tree farm, located north of Echo Lake, stated to a local newspaper that he had never seen such damage since 1958. The Echo Lake area was one of the most hard hit: four houses had minor structure damage, one truck and trailer were totaled, and one mobile home had moderate to severe damage. Dozens of trees, mostly pines and firs, some estimated to be 100 feet tall and three feet wide, were reported to have fallen in this area. One resident was sleeping when a tree fell onto his house into his bedroom, but escaped unharmed. Sporadic tree damage and power outages were reported in the Columbia Falls to Lake Blaine areas. There were dozens of fallen large trees, as well as broken power poles and lines, along Highway 83 between Ferndale and Swan Lake. One resident reported at least 10 fallen trees on their property, with one 80 foot tree damaging their garage. Multiple trees, some snapped in half and some uprooted, went down over Highway 35 between mile-markers 19 and 23, causing damage to telephone and power lines. This also caused the highway to be closed for two hours. Several homes were damaged and in one case, a large tree smashed through the roof and lower room of a home, but didn't cause any injuries. Three large trees, up to three feet in diameter, took out a power line on Finley Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most damaging northeast wind events in the past several decades occurred across western Montana. Wind speeds nearing 70 mph caused acres of downed trees and subsequent power and residential infrastructure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple locations along the western foothills of the Whitefish Range that were impacted by the high winds. At the end of Burma Road, residents estimated at least 5 acres of pine and fir trees were blown down. This caused some roof damage to several homes. A metal roof partially peeled off of a trailer home located due west, across US-93 on Acres Lane. Billboard signs were blown down between Eureka and the Canadian Border. The utility company reported two power poles damaged, and power was out for 20 hours. In the Glen Lake area, five miles east of Eureka, countless trees were strewn all over the roads, especially along Stevens and Therriault roads. At one point, emergency vehicles couldn't immediately get to a person who was on oxygen. A power company reported that they lost 8 span of power line and 8 power poles in the Graves Creek drainage area, where they reported that at least 1500 yards by 500 yards of trees blown down. They were cleaning up the damage for a few weeks after the event. They estimated that 20 log truck loads would come out of there. Multiple trees in the Dickey Lake area also had fallen on power lines. The highway department reported dozens of trees down five miles south of Troy, or between mile-markers 30 and 32 on Highway 56. A tree fell on a utility truck in this area, but no one was hurt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front moves southward. The associated northerly wind shift and cold air mass behind the front cause temperatures to drop significantly. Rain, then snow and freezing precipitation/fog expected, along with icy surfaces and reduced visibility. This event forecast as widespread with the greatest impacts felt through central and north central Montana. In general, 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulation forecast in the lower elevations from Lewis and Clark and Meagher Counties and south. North of that line, lower elevation snow accumulation forecast to range from 2 to 8 inches with the higher snow totals occurring in Fergus and Chouteau Counties and north. Higher elevation snow accumulations range from 6 to 12 inches. This front continues to cause wintery weather through Sunday as it passes from north to south and then eastward out of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust of 68 mph measured at mesonet station delmt 15.2 SW Dupuyer (mcscn).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial wave that brought the first round of snow to southwest and northcentral Montana continues to produce moderate to heavy snowfall over parts of northern Montana, as it tracks northward. The second round of snow begins to track into the area from south to north, bringing widespread snowy conditions to both northcentral and southwest Montana from Friday afternoon through Saturday morning. A Winter Storm Warning is issued though Saturday morning for Blaine, Hill, Chouteau, Cascade, |Fergus, Meagher, Gallatin, and Judith Basin Counties, as this system is expected to bring moderate to heavy snowfall to these areas and cause difficult travel conditions. There is also a Winter Weather Advisory in effect through Saturday for Glacier, Toole, Liberty, Pondera, Lewis & Clark, Jefferson, Broadwater, Madison, and Beaverhead Counties for a similar set up but with lower expected snowfall totals. Snow begins to taper off later Saturday morning from northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Choteau, fresh snowfall of 7 inches measured over the last 24 hours. With previous storm of Nov 27-28, settled snow depth is about 8 inches. Combined three-day total is about 15 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most snowfall amounts across central and southern Valley County ranged from 6.0 to 6.5 inches, especially near and south of US Highway 2. The greatest amount of 6.5 inches was reported at the Stoneberg Horse Ranch via Facebook.  Times are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across northern Phillips County ranged from 4.0 to 7.5 inches.  The greatest amount of 7.5 inches was reported by a trained spotter 30 N Dodson.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system from the Pacific coast, moved eastward through the central Rocky Mountains on a track which pulled in enough warm air from the south to allow a snow and freezing rain mix which impacted many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, freezing drizzle with ice accumulation of a quarter inch to a half inch just north of Lustre.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushes southward through Central MT. Temperatures in the|50s/60s in Southwest MT fall back into the 30s behind the cold front by late afternoon/evening. A brief period of dense fog will accompany the frontal passage. Snow continues to affect mainly Central and North Central MT for this event, with the heaviest snowfall falling along a line from Havre to Lewistown. With wet pavement surfaces likely freeze overnight, impacts to roadways are expected, even in areas that only receive about a half inch of snowfall. A winter weather advisory will continues into Wednesday morning. Expect below normal temperatures on Wednesday over much of the region, with the |warmest afternoon temperatures likely over Southwest MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from NWS Office WSW Great Falls. Glaze of ice on all area roads and sidewalks with severe driving conditions due to black ice. Great Falls International airport struggling to keep the runway open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer air moves into the region. The warm air is slow to move into the Havre area. Expect increasing winds over North Central MT, with the strongest winds over the Rocky Mountain Front/Cut Bank area. Overall, wind speeds are forecast to remain below warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust measured at mesonet station 629002 1 ENE E Glacier Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front from the north combined with a moisture feed from the Pacific to direct accumulating snow to portions of northeast Montana - primarily along the Montana/Canadian border where up to a foot of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 8 inches were common across central Valley County, especially north of US Highway 2.  The greatest amount of 8 inches was reported via Facebook on the southeast side of the town of Larslan.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shower activity on the lee side of the Rocky Mountains moved eastward and brought stronger winds aloft all the way down to the surface and spread through many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lufborough Hill MT-200 DOT site in western Garfield County measured a 58 mph wind gust at 2:03 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of higher heights is located in the Pacific Ocean off of the west coast of California. With the associated ridge axis moving on shore, height gradients are |tightening and a westerly flow is setting up across the Treasure State. High winds develop along the Rocky Mountain Front as well as over the plains of North Central Montana. These high winds continue throughout the day on Sunday. Ridgetop winds across the Northern Rockies may exceed 60 mph. Additional downslope acceleration could favor 80 mph winds at the surface at favored locations along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph may be possible elsewhere across central/north-central Montana by this afternoon. There is a chance for some precipitation, today; however, most moisture will evaporate before reaching the ground at lower levels. The best chance for precipitation occurs over the Continental Divide, as well as the central mountain ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust. Weather underground site - 52 Meltwater cabin 17 WSW Bynum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong winds expected across much of the northern and central plains|along with the Rocky Mountain Front. As the gradient increases and another clipper system moves through, expect stronger winds with the second round. Widespread gusts of 60 MPH and higher are expected, with perhaps some gusts approaching 90 MPH along The Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at mesonet station delmt 15.2 SW Dupuyer (mcscn).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of higher heights is located in the Pacific Ocean off of the west coast of California. With the associated ridge axis moving on shore, height gradients are |tightening and a westerly flow is setting up across the Treasure State. High winds develop along the Rocky Mountain Front as well as over the plains of North Central Montana. These high winds continue throughout the day on Sunday. Ridgetop winds across the Northern Rockies may exceed 60 mph. Additional downslope acceleration could favor 80 mph winds at the surface at favored locations along the Rocky Mountain Front. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph may be possible elsewhere across central/north-central Montana by this afternoon. There is a chance for some precipitation, today; however, most moisture will evaporate before reaching the ground at lower levels. The best chance for precipitation occurs over the Continental Divide, as well as the central mountain ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at mesonet station mtm76 4 E Cascade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft brought mixed precipitation to a large portion of the Billings Forecast Area. Warm temperatures aloft allowed the precipitation to fall in liquid form, but surface temperatures at or slightly below freezing, resulted in the precipitation to freeze upon contact. This resulted in widespread freezing drizzle and freezing rain. As a result, numerous accidents were reported across the area. Over a span of 16 hours, 102 car crashes were reported to the Montana Highway Patrol. The crashes resulted in numerous injuries and resulted in a fatality in Sweet Grass County. Most of the crashes occurred across the Billings, Laurel, Columbus, Big Timber, Red Lodge and Hardin areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and black ice were reported across the area. A fatal car crash occurred in Sweet Grass County at about 10:01 am on I-90 west of Big Timber near mile marker 364. The rollover crash occurred after the driver lost control on the icy roadway and entered the center median, rolling once. The driver, a 57-year-old man from Georgia, was not wearing a seat belt and he was ejected from his SUV during the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers in weakly unstable and moist northwest flow continue through the evening hours, with most areas seeing at least a slight chance of snow. A vort max embedded in the flow moves across the area Monday evening through Tuesday morning. Some models indicate this could bring a localized area of better snow across |central portions. Confidence is only moderate in this scenario, and snowfall from this potential could amount to a quick inch of snow that may impact those traveling overnight or during Tuesday's morning commute. Overnight temperatures will be near normal and generally in the teens and 20s. Some pockets of patchy fog are also possible, mainly for northern and central portions. Morning snow showers diminish somewhat Tuesday afternoon. A switch to southerly flow could then produce some more isolated to scattered snow showers for Tuesday, mainly for mountain areas. Highs on Tuesday will also be slightly cooler and generally in the 20s and 30s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snow of 7 inches measured. Snow began at 6 pm Monday evening with measurement taken and reported at noon on Tuesday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial wave that brought the first round of snow to southwest and northcentral Montana continues to produce moderate to heavy snowfall over parts of northern Montana, as it tracks northward. The second round of snow begins to track into the area from south to north, bringing widespread snowy conditions to both northcentral and southwest Montana from Friday afternoon through Saturday morning. A Winter Storm Warning is issued though Saturday morning for Blaine, Hill, Chouteau, Cascade, |Fergus, Meagher, Gallatin, and Judith Basin Counties, as this system is expected to bring moderate to heavy snowfall to these areas and cause difficult travel conditions. There is also a Winter Weather Advisory in effect through Saturday for Glacier, Toole, Liberty, Pondera, Lewis & Clark, Jefferson, Broadwater, Madison, and Beaverhead Counties for a similar set up but with lower expected snowfall totals. Snow begins to taper off later Saturday morning from northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow depth 10 inches, mostly new overnight. 11 W Helena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shower activity on the lee side of the Rocky Mountains moved eastward and brought stronger winds aloft all the way down to the surface and spread through many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, two separate high wind events occurred on the 24th.  For the first one, sustained winds of at least 40 mph began at 4:37 AM and ended at 10:30 AM.  The first severe wind gust of 63 mph was measured at 4:46 AM.  The peak wind gusts of 66 mph were measured from 6:45 AM through 6:48 AM.  For the second event, sustained winds of at least 40 mph began at 11:45 AM and ended at 2:00 PM.  The first severe wind gust of 63 mph was measured at 12:15 PM.  The peak wind gusts of 63 mph were measured from 12:15 PM through 12:20 PM and from 12:40 PM through 12:45 PM.  Severe wind gusts of 58 mph were also measured in the town of Zortman at 6:00 AM and 1:15 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions develop as a mesoscale band of heavy snow develops along a surface boundary emerging off of The Rocky Mountain Front. Temperatures drop into the single digits behind the boundary, while wind gusts exceed 45 mph at many locations. Mesoscale models place this band of snow right along the eastern edge of The Rocky Mountain Front, but it forms and becomes nearly stationary along, or just west of I-15. Snowfall rates within this band are expected near two inches per hour, resulting in 6 to 9 inches of new snow in just a few hours. Although not quite in the right location, most of the high resolution model guidance weakens this boundary late in the evening. The large scale upper level low responsible for this event becomes stationary over southwest Oregon. Embedded shortwave features continue to rotate over the region and producing nearly continuous, widespread snow through Thursday. Snowfall intensity decreases as the localized snowband weakens, but snow becomes generally more widespread. Snow-water ratios remain high, meaning this is a powder snow that will blow easily and cause reduced visibility and drifting problems across many roads with light to moderate winds. Winds increase across the region as the surface low tracks through southern Idaho on Thursday. Snowfall dissipates on Thursday night with shortwave ridging aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 7 inches over Augusta, MT. Relayed through ham radio operator.
